docs.title: Governance Lifecycle
docs.text:
The Continuous Architecture System manages the full lifecycle of architectural knowledge.

Lifecycle Stage Artifact Count 1. Pattern Proposals 6 2. Promotion Recommendations 3 3. Promotion Decisions 1 4. ADRs Pending Review 1 5. ADRs Approved 9 6. Institutional Patterns 19

docs.title: The Problem
docs.text: Traditional enterprise architecture relies heavily on static documentation to govern complex systems. While these artifacts capture initial strategic intent, they are inherently disconnected from the engineering reality. As systems evolve, a critical gap forms between the documented architecture and the actual implementation, leading to severe documentation drift and a loss of alignment. The fundamental problem is that static governance mechanisms cannot scale to meet the demands of modern delivery velocity without introducing unacceptable friction.

docs.title: Why Traditional Governance Breaks
docs.text: Architecture governance typically degrades not because of engineering failure, but due to systemic operational limitations. Manual review processes and point-in-time assessments are fundamentally incompatible with continuous delivery and agile execution. As an organisation scales, the administrative burden of maintaining architectural alignment across disconnected teams becomes unsustainable. This friction inevitably leads to architectural drift, where the system's operational reality diverges from strategic intent, creating hidden technical debt and operational risk.

docs.title: Shift-Left Governance
docs.text:
Traditional governance mechanisms operate too late in the delivery lifecycle, relying on post-implementation reviews, documentation reconciliation, and periodic oversight to detect architectural divergence. CAS shifts architecture governance directly into the software construction process itself, establishing preventive operational guardrails that guide implementation from the moment code is created while continuously detecting and correcting what still escapes those boundaries.

This creates a dual-governance operational model:

  • Preventive Governance — Governance aware onboarding, CAS Skills, coding agent operational guidance, specification-driven development workflows, and CI validation establish architectural boundaries before implementation diverges.
  • Corrective Governance — Continuous governance workflows, capability extraction, steering projection, evaluation, and operational reconciliation detect and correct architectural drift that still emerges during system evolution.

By embedding governance directly into software construction workflows, CAS transforms architecture from a passive review function into an active operational capability that continuously shapes repository evolution in real time. This governance aware software construction model becomes increasingly critical in environments where coding agents actively participate in delivery workflows.

docs.title: The Architectural Insight
docs.text: The resolution to this drift lies in shifting the paradigm from static documentation and reactive governance toward operational intelligence embedded directly into the software construction lifecycle. The core insight behind the Continuous Architecture System (CAS) is that architecture must operate as a living system—where intent is continuously reconciled against the repository state. By treating Architecture-as-Code, governance evolves from a periodic, manual review process into a continuous, reproducible operational feedback loop that guarantees alignment while preserving auditability, replayability, and governance trust.

docs.title: The CAS Approach
docs.text: CAS operationalises architecture governance by establishing a governance aware operational intelligence system capable of continuously reconciling operational reality against architectural intent. At its core, it relies on auditable capability extraction—analysing repository evolution to derive normalised operational capabilities without relying on stochastic governance decisions. Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) serve as the single, machine-readable source of truth, ensuring that governance remains reproducible, auditable, and operationally trustworthy. CAS also establishes governance aware operational workflows for both engineering teams and coding agents, enabling architecture intent to participate directly in the software construction process itself. This operational model establishes the foundation for governance aware coding agent collaboration, where governed operational foundations participate directly in automated software construction workflows. Through CAS Skills, specification-driven development workflows, operational onboarding contracts, and governance aware CI validation, the system prevents significant categories of architectural drift before they materialise while still continuously detecting and correcting what escapes those preventive boundaries. Through continuous operational feedback loops, the system automatically detects structural anomalies, governance drift, and operational capability evolution, empowering architects to manage governance through enablement and automation rather than manual constraint.

docs.title: Operational Outcomes
docs.text: The implementation of CAS fundamentally reduces architectural drift by combining preventive governance mechanisms with continuous operational visibility into the system's structural integrity. This approach increases governance scalability, allowing small architecture teams to oversee massive transformation initiatives safely. By establishing reproducible feedback loops and mandatory architectural guardrails, CAS improves the alignment between executive strategy, engineering delivery, and coding agent execution, ensuring that continuous transformation remains operationally sustainable, governance aware, and architecturally coherent.

docs.title: Broader Implications
docs.text: CAS represents a necessary maturity evolution in how enterprise architecture operates. It signals the transition from architecture as a passive recording function toward architecture as a continuous operational governance system. This operational governance integration demonstrates that complex governance can be operationalised through reproducible intelligence, continuous feedback loops, and governance aware automation without sacrificing strategic optionality or operational safety. By combining governed operational foundations with automated operational interpretation, CAS establishes the foundation for scalable Governance-as-Code in increasingly automated engineering ecosystems. Ultimately, it provides the operational infrastructure required for sustainable enterprise transformation in an era of growing technological and organisational complexity.

docs.title: Related Narrative
docs.text: The operational implications of governance aware software construction become even more significant in highly automated delivery environments where coding agents actively participate in implementation workflows. The companion narrative Governance Aware Coding Agent Collaboration expands on how CAS establishes governed operational foundations, preventive operational controls, and continuous governance reconciliation mechanisms for scalable coding agent ecosystems.

docs.title: Closing Reflection
docs.text: Architecture should never be an isolated review exercise; it must become an active operational capability that participates directly in software delivery. By shifting governance left into the software construction process itself while continuously reconciling repository evolution against architectural intent, CAS closes the gap between strategic direction and engineering reality. The Continuous Architecture System demonstrates that true operational sustainability emerges when architecture evolves from static documentation into a living, measurable, governance aware operational system capable of both preventing and correcting architectural entropy continuously.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: Implement the Governance Health and Intelligence Dashboard using a layered architecture and a deterministic heuristic health model.

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
• Source: specs/030-governance-health-intelligence-dashboard/spec.md
  • Decision classification: system

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: The CAS portal lacks a central landing experience that synthesizes governance health. Users must read individual ADRs, Architecture Work items, and Alignment Reports to understand the system state, which is inefficient for high-level monitoring and risk identification.

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
1. Layered Decoupling: Separating data access (data_access.py), health logic (health_model.py), and the web interface ensures the health engine can be tested independently of the UI.
  2. Heuristic Model (Phase 1): Using simple, transparent heuristics (e.g., Red status if high-severity findings exist) ensures trust and allows for incremental refinement.
  3. Read-Only Intelligence: The dashboard serves as a consumption layer. It derives insights but never becomes a new source of truth, preserving the ADR as the authoritative record.
  4. Information Scent: Providing summaries with direct links to artifacts (Traceability) ensures users can verify insights without friction.

docs.title: Next Step
docs.text:
• Implement the cas_runner/dashboard/ module structure.
  • Define specific heuristic triggers for Green/Amber/Red states in the health model.
  • Replace the portal homepage with the synthesized dashboard view.

docs.title: Traceability
docs.text: Artefact ID Specification 030-governance-health-intelligence-dashboard Pattern heuristic-driven-arbitration Pattern contract-first-architecture Pattern structured-output-via-schema

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: Adopt the Runtime Capability Inventory (inventory.yaml) as the project-specific representation of current implemented capabilities. It is initialized via a one-time bootstrap script (scanning the repository using a git diff against the empty tree hash) and kept up-to-date incrementally during Capability Extraction.

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
• Source: specs/062-cas-runtime-capability-bootstrap/spec.md
  • Decision classification: system

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Downstream governance workflows like Architecture Work Assessment and Governance Context Builder require a complete view of currently implemented runtime capabilities. Scraping raw commit deltas directly is insufficient because deltas only describe recent changes, meaning pre-existing or unchanged capabilities are omitted.

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
1. State vs Evolution Separation: Separation of concerns between change extraction (deltas) and current state tracking (inventory).
  2. Deterministic Bootstrapping: Reuses the git-extractor diff mechanism against the empty tree hash 4b825dc642cb6eb9a0f94140d12a1d9a3435b1d57, guaranteeing identical parsing rules to standard commits.
  3. Traceability Preservation: Retired capabilities are flagged as retired instead of deleted, preserving historical references.
  4. Direct Consumers: Allows Context Builder to load all capability evidence instantly from one single file instead of performing costly codebase scans.

docs.title: Traceability
docs.text: Artefact ID Specification 062-cas-runtime-capability-bootstrap Pattern single-source-of-truth Pattern incremental-evolution Pattern structured-output-via-schema

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: Adopt deterministic-generator-contract as a mandatory standard for all CAS generators.

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
• Source: aw-ideas-to-life-cas-2026-04-24-02
  • Finding: ALIGN-005
  • AEM Source: aem-ideas-to-life-cas-2026-04-26-01
  • Decision classification: system

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: CAS generators were producing inconsistent outputs across runtimes and model executions because naming, versioning, and persistence rules were not explicit. This created drift in generated artefacts and made repeated execution difficult to reason about.

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
The contract is required to:

  1. make generator outputs repeatable
  2. enforce explicit naming and versioning
  3. require persistence and date scoping
  4. reduce nondeterministic behaviour across model changes
  5. make generator output reviewable and traceable

docs.title: Next Step
docs.text:
• Enforce the contract in generator prompts.
  • Validate compliance through alignment checks.
  • Update related artefacts that depend on generator output format or persistence rules.

docs.title: Problem
docs.text:
The project diagram set still references aem-ideas-to-life-cas-2026-04-22-v2 and does not include last_verified metadata in frontmatter.

This creates drift between the diagram layer and the currently governed architecture state.

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
The diagram governance blueprint requires diagrams to be:

  1. persistent
  2. traceable
  3. governed
  4. continuously validated

The current diagram set already serves the correct behavioural and structural purpose, but its traceability metadata is incomplete.

Updating the diagram set now will:

  • align diagrams with the current AEM
  • keep diagrams consistent with ADR and system design
  • make future validation deterministic
  • reduce the risk of stale or misleading architectural views

docs.title: Next Step
docs.text:
• Update all project diagram frontmatter blocks to reference aem-ideas-to-life-cas-2026-04-22-v3
  • Add last_verified: "2026-04-22" to each diagram frontmatter block
  • Re-run diagram validation against cas-diagram-governance

docs.title: ADR: Adopt Hybrid LLM Gateway Integration
ADR: Adopt Hybrid LLM Gateway Integration
Context
docs.text: The CAS system currently uses a stubbed LLM provider. To support real-world architectural flows, we need to integrate with multiple providers (Gemini, OpenAI, etc.) while ensuring that outputs are machine-readable and system stability is maintained.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: We will implement a unified LLM Gateway in src/cas_runner/llm/ that: 1. Defines a provider-agnostic LLMClient protocol. 2. Uses LiteLLM for normalized multi-provider execution. 3. Mandates Pydantic schemas for all reasoning steps. 4. Implements robust JSON extraction to handle model "chatter".

docs.title: Consequences
docs.text:
• Pros: Provider agnosticism, strict type safety, reduced boilerplate for adding new models.
  • Cons: Adds litellm and pydantic as core dependencies.
  • Risks: Potential for validation failures if models deviate from complex schemas.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: Define and implement a Pattern Classification Framework to govern the scope and promotion path of architectural patterns and rules within the Continuous Architecture System.

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
• Source: alignment-report-ideas-to-life-cas-2026-04-22-02.md
  • Finding: ALIGN-001 (high severity)
  • Violated Rules: rule-decisions-must-be-persisted, rule-adr-single-source-of-truth

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: The AEM roadmap included "Pattern Classification Framework" as a NEXT item, but this transition lacked a formal decision record. Without a defined framework, the distinction between core, supporting, and project-specific patterns remains subjective, leading to potential knowledge drift and rule bloating in the global layer.

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text: A formal classification framework is necessary to: 1. Provide objective criteria for promoting a pattern from project-specific to global scope. 2. Define the precedence and isolation boundaries between global rules and project-level constraints. 3. Ensure that the knowledge layer remains modular and scalable as more projects are added to the CAS. 4. Support the "System Proposes, Architect Decides" rule by providing the architect with a clear classification rubric.

docs.title: Next Step
docs.text:
• Define specific criteria for "global" vs "project" vs "step-specific" rule and pattern classification.
  • Update the pattern-scope-classifier.v1 prompt to reflect the new framework.
  • Audit existing patterns in knowledge/global/patterns/ against the new criteria.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Governance rules must not be duplicated inside prompts
  • Prompts must defer to global rules for system behaviour
  • Execution rules are allowed and expected within prompts
  • The architecture-intent-generator.v2.md has clean separation - mostly execution rules
  • The architecture-work-to-evolution-map.v1.md has significant governance rule duplication
  • The rules-conflict-detector.v1.md has appropriate execution-level behaviour rules only

docs.title: issue-aem-001
docs.text: type: "misplacement" severity: "warning" prompt: "architecture-work-to-evolution-map.v1.md" description: > Rule "Apply governance rules from the available rule context" is a governance-level directive. System-wide rule application should be centralised, not embedded in a prompt. rule_text: > Apply governance rules from the available rule context. related_global_rule: "rule-precedence-hierarchy" resolution: action: "move" target: "global_rule" explanation: > This directive describes how governance hierarchy is applied. It is a system-wide constraint applicable across multiple prompts and should live in knowledge/global/rules/.

docs.title: issue-ppw-001
docs.text: type: "misplacement" severity: "warning" prompt: "pattern-promotion-workflow.v1.md" description: > "Do NOT assume authority to finalize architectural decisions" is a governance constraint about human-in-the-loop control. It defines system behaviour across the promotion workflow. rule_text: > Do NOT assume authority to finalize architectural decisions. related_global_rule: "rule-system-proposes-architect-decides" resolution: action: "move" target: "global_rule" explanation: > This governance constraint should be centralised. It defines who has authority over promotion decisions — a system-wide behaviour not specific to this prompt.

docs.text: type: "misplacement" severity: "warning" prompt: "pattern-promotion-workflow.v1.md" description: > "The system only proposes; an architect decides" is already captured in rule-system-proposes-architect-decides. Prompt-level restatement creates duplication risk. rule_text: > The system only proposes; an architect decides. related_global_rule: "rule-system-proposes-architect-decides" resolution: action: "move" target: "global_rule" explanation: > This governance rule is already centralised. Prompt should reference the global rule instead of restating it.

docs.title: issue-adr-001
docs.text: type: "misplacement" severity: "warning" prompt: "promotion-to-decision-records.v1.md" description: > "Ingest rules from both global and project scopes" is a governance directive about rule loading behaviour. It belongs in global rules or a process standard. rule_text: > Ingest rules from both global and project scopes. related_global_rule: "rule-precedence-hierarchy" resolution: action: "move" target: "global_rule" explanation: > The obligation to load and apply rules is a system-wide governance directive, not a prompt-specific behaviour. Should be in knowledge/global/rules/.

System-wide rule application should be centralised. rule_text: > Apply governance rules from the available rule context. related_global_rule: "rule-precedence-hierarchy" resolution: action: "move" target: "global_rule" explanation: > This directive belongs in knowledge/global/rules/. It defines how the system interprets and applies governance hierarchy across all prompts.

docs.title: issue-aid-002
docs.text: type: "misplacement" severity: "warning" prompt: "architecture-intent-generator.v2.md" description: > "Rules must influence interpretation but must NOT be treated as patterns or decisions" is a governance-level separation principle. It defines how rules interact with patterns and decisions — a system-wide constraint. rule_text: > Rules must influence interpretation but must NOT be treated as patterns or decisions. related_global_rule: "rule-pattern-decision-lifecycle-alignment" resolution: action: "move" target: "global_rule" explanation: > This defines the separation between rules, patterns, and decisions — a governance invariant that applies system-wide, not just in this prompt.

docs.title: issue-h2p-001
docs.text: type: "misplacement" severity: "blocking" prompt: "heuristic-to-pattern.v1 (needs review).md" description: > "Apply governance rules from the available rule context" is a system-wide governance directive embedded in a single prompt. rule_text: > Apply governance rules from the available rule context. related_global_rule: "rule-precedence-hierarchy" resolution: action: "move" target: "global_rule" explanation: > This directive should be centralised. It defines how governance is applied across the system.

docs.title: issue-h2p-002
docs.text: type: "misplacement" severity: "blocking" prompt: "heuristic-to-pattern.v1 (needs review).md" description: > Three rules define pattern lifecycle constraints for heuristic-derived patterns: scope.type default, promotion.candidate always true, origin.promoted_from population. These are governance rules about pattern lifecycle — they should be centralised. rule_text: > - Do NOT generate patterns with scope.type = "core" in this step - Always set promotion.candidate = true for heuristic-derived patterns - Always populate origin.promoted_from with the source signal ID(s) related_global_rule: "rule-patterns-born-as-supporting" resolution: action: "move" target: "global_rule" explanation: > These rules govern the lifecycle of heuristic-derived patterns. They are system-wide constraints that should live in knowledge/global/rules/, not in this single prompt. The prompt should reference the global rule.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Focused re-audit of 5 prompts with previously detected issues
  • 8 total issues found: 2 blocking (heuristic-to-pattern), 6 warnings
  • All issues are of type misplacement — governance directives embedded in prompts
  • Related global rules already exist for most: rule-precedence-hierarchy, rule-system-proposes-architect-decides, rule-patterns-born-as-supporting
  • Recommended action: consolidate "apply governance rules" directive into a single global rule to avoid duplication across multiple prompts
  • heuristic-to-pattern has the most governance misplacements (5 rules) and should be prioritised for cleanup

docs.title: Rule conflicts ideas to life cas 2026 04 21 01
CONFLICT-001
docs.text:
description: > Three global rules define overlapping mappings between decision.status and lifecycle stages: - rule-decision-status-drives-stage defines status→stage constraints - rule-pattern-decision-lifecycle-alignment defines pattern maturity vs decision status interpretation - rule-aem-consistency-with-decisions defines AEM-specific status→stage mappings These rules duplicate the same conceptual mapping in slightly different contexts, creating maintenance risk.

type: "duplication"

severity: "warning"

sources: global_rule: "rule-decision-status-drives-stage"

rules_involved: - "rule-decision-status-drives-stage" - "rule-pattern-decision-lifecycle-alignment" - "rule-aem-consistency-with-decisions"

context: affected_items: - "architecture_evolution_map" - "patterns" - "decision records" affected_stage: "governance"

resolution: strategy: "manual_review" applied_rule: "rule-decision-status-drives-stage" outcome: > rule-decision-status-drives-stage is selected as the canonical source for status→stage mapping as it is the most explicit and constraint-focused. The other rules should reference this rule rather than redefining the mapping. Consider consolidating into a single source of truth.

impact: description: > Maintaining three separate rule files with overlapping mappings increases risk of drift. Updates to status→stage logic must be made in multiple places. Recommendation: create a single canonical decision-status-schema rule that others reference.

docs.title: CONFLICT-002
docs.text:
description: > rule-decision-schema-must-be-structured requires all decisions to use structured YAML with fields: decision.proposal, rationale.summary, evidence.strength, and confidence. However, rule-decision-status-drives-stage, rule-pattern-decision-lifecycle-alignment, and rule-aem-consistency-with-decisions do not follow this schema format in their frontmatter. This is a meta-inconsistency where rules don't follow their own rules.

type: "inconsistency"

severity: "warning"

sources: global_rule: "rule-decision-schema-must-be-structured"

rules_involved: - "rule-decision-schema-must-be-structured" - "rule-decision-status-drives-stage" - "rule-pattern-decision-lifecycle-alignment" - "rule-aem-consistency-with-decisions"

context: affected_items: - "rule definitions" affected_stage: "quality assurance"

resolution: strategy: "manual_review" applied_rule: "rule-decision-schema-must-be-structured" outcome: > The three non-compliant rule files should be reformatted to follow the structured schema. This is a documentation hygiene issue, not a functional conflict. The rules' content is valid but their representation is inconsistent with the established schema standard.

impact: description: > Inconsistent rule formatting prevents automated processing of rule metadata. Machine readability of the rule set is degraded. Standardizing on the structured schema will enable validation, automated querying, and better integration with AEM generation.

docs.title: CONFLICT-003
docs.text:
description: > The file knowledge/global/rules/rule-aem-derived-from-decisions.md contains a frontmatter id of "rule-aem-consistency-with-decisions", which does not match the filename. This creates ambiguity when referencing the rule by ID vs by file path. Additionally, this creates potential confusion with rule-aem-derived-from-decisions expectations.

type: "ambiguity"

severity: "warning"

sources: global_rule: "rule-aem-derived-from-decisions"

rules_involved: - "rule-aem-derived-from-decisions" - "rule-aem-consistency-with-decisions"

context: affected_items: - "rule identification" affected_stage: "maintenance"

resolution: strategy: "manual_review" applied_rule: "rule-aem-consistency-with-decisions" outcome: > The ID in the file frontmatter (rule-aem-consistency-with-decisions) should be considered the canonical identifier. The filename should be updated to match, or the ID should be updated to match the filename. Recommendation: rename file to rule-aem-consistency-with-decisions.md to align with the ID used in the frontmatter.

impact: description: > Filename/ID mismatch creates ambiguity in rule references. Automated systems may fail to locate rules when using ID-based lookups. Manual references may point to wrong files. Correcting the alignment will improve system reliability and reduce maintenance confusion.

docs.title: Rule conflicts ideas to life cas 2026 04 21 03
CONFLICT-001
docs.text:
description: > Two global rules use non-standard quotation marks ("smart quotes"/curly quotes) in their YAML frontmatter id fields, which may cause parsing failures or ID mismatches in automated systems expecting standard ASCII quotes. The affected rules are: - rule-aem-consistency-with-decisions.md: id: "rule-aem-consistency-with-decisions" - rule-pattern-decision-lifecycle-alignment.md: id: "rule-pattern-decision-lifecycle-alignment" All other rules use standard straight quotation marks (").

type: "inconsistency"

severity: "warning"

sources: global_rule: "rule-decision-schema-must-be-structured"

rules_involved: - "rule-aem-consistency-with-decisions" - "rule-pattern-decision-lifecycle-alignment"

context: affected_items: - "rule frontmatter parsing" - "automated rule indexing" affected_stage: "quality"

resolution: strategy: "manual_review" applied_rule: "rule-decision-schema-must-be-structured" outcome: > The frontmatter id fields in both affected rule files should be updated to use standard straight quotation marks (") instead of curly/smart quotes (") to ensure consistent parsing across all YAML processing tools. This aligns with the structured schema requirement for machine-readable formats.

impact: description: > Non-standard quotes may cause YAML parsers to fail or interpret the id field incorrectly. This could lead to rules not being indexed, referenced incorrectly, or excluded from automated governance processes. Standardizing on ASCII quotes ensures compatibility.

docs.title: System Evolution
docs.text: The system is evolving from a template-driven documentation model to a runtime-native Architecture Intelligence Platform. Key changes include the abstraction of the Knowledge Provider to decouple logic from storage, the shift to a standardized Workflow Execution Model (Runner/Workflow/Provider), and the elevation of Architecture Work as the authoritative planning registry. Manual governance is transitioning toward an automated Detect-Analyse-Execute loop to address a 15:1 governance velocity bottleneck.

docs.title: Intent & Direction
docs.text: CAS is trending toward 'Continuous Architecture Governance' with a focus on 'Architecture Intelligence'. Future intent centers on end-to-end traceability from Business Intent artifacts to Code implementation, cross-project portfolio intelligence via a Knowledge Graph, and real-time operational governance surfaces. Integration with external enterprise architecture ecosystems and automated drift remediation are primary future milestones.

docs.title: System Evolution
docs.text:
CAS has evolved from a deterministic transformation pipeline into a governed semantic reasoning infrastructure. The system now enforces strict input scoping, two-phase diagram handling, and a proposal-decision separation invariant. Orchestration is driven by a ReasoningExecutionContract that mandates schema validation and separates reasoning from rendering. Governance is centralized via a rules-context-loader and conflict detector, establishing a global > project > prompt precedence hierarchy. Observability is integrated through OTEL-compatible telemetry, capturing reasoning lineage, token economics, and provider behavior. The system operates on a two-phase model: design-time governance (CAS Skills) and runtime orchestration (FlowRunner), ensuring deterministic execution while supporting LLM-backed reasoning steps.

CAS has now entered a measurable architecture cognition phase where orchestration quality, governance reasoning, semantic contracts, prompt lineage, and provider economics are observable and evaluable. The platform is evolving toward a closed Prevent ↔ Detect ↔ Feedback governance loop integrating CAS Skills, orchestration workflows, evaluation harnesses, and future CI/CD audit pipelines. Strategic focus has shifted from foundational orchestration refactoring toward ReasoningOps, GovernanceOps, adaptive orchestration policies, and operational architecture governance.

docs.title: Intent & Direction
docs.text:
CAS is transitioning from an architecture extraction system to an Architecture Intelligence Platform. The vision emphasizes connecting business intent, architecture, code, and decisions through continuous alignment and traceability. Future directions include business layer integration (BRD ingestion), code intelligence for structural analysis, and enterprise ecosystem integration (EAM tools). The system aims to support capability-aware model routing, adaptive reasoning optimization, and an evaluation-driven improvement loop. Human-in-the-loop governance remains core, with automation proposing and architects deciding. Visual interaction layers (AIM) and graph-native knowledge representation are planned for future exploration.

Near-term strategic priorities focus on operationalizing continuous architecture governance through CI/CD-integrated audit workflows, orchestration economics visibility, validation governance telemetry, and lightweight capability-aware routing. Longer-term direction includes GovernanceOps workflows for pattern evolution, governance drift management, architecture intelligence integration across business and code layers, and external validation of CAS as a continuous architecture governance platform.

docs.text: Id Title Forum Status Summary adr-aw-ideas-to-life-cas-2026-05-31-001 Architecture Work Registry as Authoritative Architectural Backlog accepted Establish Architecture Work as the authoritative architectural change backlog for CAS. AEM becomes a generated dashboard summarizing state rather than owning planning information. adr-aw-ideas-to-life-cas-20260605-04 CAS Workflow Execution Model accepted Adopt a standardized model where CAS Runner orchestrates and workflows reason. Decouples orchestration from reasoning to ensure consistency and portability. adr-aw-ideas-to-life-cas-20260614-1 Continuous Architecture Governance Platform Evolution pending Institutionalize a formal Governance Lifecycle, adopt unified Workflow Execution Model, establish Rules as authoritative constraints, and introduce a Governance Action Framework. adr-aw-ideas-to-life-cas-2026-06-06-003 Integration of Business Intent into Architectural Reasoning Loop pending Formalise the Business Intent artifact schema and integrate it as a mandatory input for the Alignment Engine to ensure strategic alignment and traceability.

docs.title: System Evolution
docs.text: The system is transitioning from a manual, documentation-heavy governance model to a runtime-native, continuous architecture governance platform. Key shifts include the institutionalization of the Architecture Work Registry as the authoritative planning layer, the adoption of a standardized Workflow Execution Model (Runner orchestrates, Workflow reasons), and the move toward automated governance loops (Detect-Analyse-Execute). Governance has shifted from periodic review to lightweight, embedded participation (ReasoningOps/GovernanceOps). Knowledge access is decoupled from repository structure via the Knowledge Provider abstraction. The platform now supports deterministic derivation through strict frontmatter contracts and structured schemas for machine consumption.

docs.title: Intent & Direction
docs.text: CAS is evolving from a decision-driven architecture system into an Architecture Intelligence Platform. The forward trajectory focuses on connecting business intent to architecture and code, enabling continuous alignment between intent (AID) and implementation reality. Future capabilities include cross-project knowledge graphs, interactive architecture intelligence maps, and external ecosystem integration. The vision emphasizes traceability, automated drift detection, and human-governed continuous evolution. Architecture intent artefacts will align with system state rather than merely reflecting it, supporting proactive prevention and detection of architectural drift.

docs.text: The system is transitioning from a semi-automated documentation model to a 'Continuous Architecture Governance' platform. Key shifts include the introduction of the Architecture Work Registry as the authoritative backlog, the decoupling of storage via the Knowledge Provider abstraction, and the standardization of the CAS Workflow Execution Model. The emergence of ReasoningOps and GovernanceOps signifies a move toward governance as a continuous delivery participant rather than a periodic review event.

docs.title: Intent & Direction
docs.text: The strategic intent is to evolve CAS into an 'Architecture Intelligence Platform' that connects Business Intent to Code Reality. This involves formalizing Business Intent schemas, implementing end-to-end traceability (Business -> Decision -> Pattern -> Code), and developing an interactive Architecture Intelligence Map (AIM). Future directions include Portfolio Intelligence for cross-project trends and a unified Knowledge Graph layer for complex architectural queries.

docs.text: The system is transitioning from a 'template-ready' manual documentation model to a 'Continuous Architecture Governance' platform. This evolution is characterized by the introduction of a standardized Workflow Execution Model (Runner/Workflow/KnowledgeProvider), the establishment of an authoritative Architecture Work Registry, and the onboarding of runtime-native capabilities (ConversationToAW, DiscoveryToAW). Governance is shifting from periodic review to a continuously active participation model (GovernanceOps/ReasoningOps) embedded in delivery.

docs.title: Intent & Direction
docs.text: The strategic intent is to transform CAS into an Architecture Intelligence Platform. Key directions include: 1) End-to-end traceability from Business Intent to Code Reality; 2) Automated Detect-Analyse-Execute loops for drift remediation; 3) Cross-project intelligence via an Architecture Knowledge Graph; and 4) Integration with the broader enterprise ecosystem (EAM tools, CI/CD). The vision prioritizes 'aligning with' system state over merely reflecting it, ensuring intent remains a proactive constraint.

Key shifts include the elevation of the Architecture Work Registry to the authoritative planning source and the abstraction of Knowledge Providers to support multi-repo/project intelligence. This involves formalising business intent schemas for automated alignment checks and developing an Architecture Knowledge Graph for cross-project impact analysis. UX intent is moving toward 'Architecture Intelligence Maps' and visual evolution timelines published via the Governance Portal.

docs.title: System Evolution
docs.text: The system is evolving from a template-driven documentation model to a runtime-native Architecture Intelligence Platform. Key shifts include the institutionalization of the Architecture Work Registry as the authoritative backlog (Rule Architecture Work Authority), the separation of reasoning and orchestration via the CAS Workflow Execution Model, and the introduction of a Knowledge Provider abstraction to decouple logic from storage. Automation is moving toward a closed-loop 'Detect-Analyse-Execute' model to address governance velocity bottlenecks.

docs.title: Intent & Direction
docs.text: The strategic direction is to connect business intent to code reality via an interactive Architecture Intelligence Layer. Future focus includes end-to-end traceability from requirements to runtime behavior, multi-hop lineage analysis via a Knowledge Graph, and visual exploration of architecture evolution through Intelligence Maps. Integration with external enterprise ecosystems is planned to prevent knowledge silos.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text: The Governance Workflow Mapping defines how the various operational workflows within the Continuous Architecture System (CAS) participate in the overarching governance model. In an AI-native delivery ecosystem, governance is not a standalone process but an emergent property of workflow participation. This mapping establishes the relationships between preventive controls, detection mechanisms, and feedback loops, ensuring that architectural intent is continuously operationalized across all delivery systems.

docs.title: Governance Participation Loops
docs.text: CAS operates through a tightly coupled Prevent ↔ Detect ↔ Feedback loop that transforms governance from a manual oversight function into a continuous operational capability: - Continuous Participation: Workflows are designed for active participation by humans, agents, and automated systems, rather than passive adherence. - Operational Intelligence Generation: Each execution of a workflow contributes to a shared pool of operational signals that inform architectural state. - Governance Signal Lifecycle: Operational signals continuously evolve from implementation observations into governance intelligence, participation adjustments, and architectural pattern evolution. - Governance-Aware Execution: The CAS Runner and associated workflows natively consume governance rules, ensuring that execution is always bounded by architectural intent.

docs.title: Prevent Workflows
docs.text: Prevent workflows are designed to constrain bad structure at the point of creation, embedding governance into the implementation lifecycle: - CAS Skills: Operational participation modules that embed architectural intent, governance boundaries, and reusable implementation patterns directly into human and agentic execution workflows. - Onboarding Governance: Operational guidance contracts (AGENTS.md, CLAUDE.md, GEMINI.md) that establish the architectural "rules of the road" for all participants. - Specification-Driven Development (SDD) Validation: CI-level checks (cas-validate-sdd.yml) that ensure no implementation changes occur without a corresponding architectural specification. - Active Constraints: The rules-context-loader ensures that every orchestration flow is governed by the latest global and project-specific architecture rules.

docs.title: Detect Workflows
docs.text: Detect workflows identify governance drift and architectural incoherence by continuously monitoring implementation evolution: - Alignment Engine: Continuously evaluates operational participation, implementation evolution, and system state against governance intent, producing high-signal architectural alignment intelligence. - Steering Drift Detection: Scans operational artifacts to identify stale references, path drift, and gaps between system reality and steering guidance. - AEM Generation: Continuously derives the Architecture Evolution Map (AEM) from authoritative decisions and recent implementation signals, providing a real-time view of system state. - CI/CD Governance: Automated validation workflows (cas-build-validation.yml) that perform structural and contract checks on every pull request. - Terminology Governance: Integrated checks within the alignment engine to ensure semantic consistency across decisions and code. - Cross-Workflow Coherence Monitoring: Detects governance drift, orchestration inconsistency, and architectural fragmentation across operational workflows.

docs.title: Feedback Workflows
docs.text: Feedback workflows return governance intelligence back into the ecosystem to influence future behavior and refine architectural direction: - Steering Projection: Translates detected drift and architectural deltas into actionable steering artifacts and controlled patch proposals. - Operational Intelligence Loops: Continuously derive governance intelligence, capability evolution awareness, and participation signals from real delivery activity to refine future operational behaviour. - GovernanceOps: The systematic management of architectural rules and patterns based on feedback from real delivery outcomes. - AID Generation: Synthesizes intent and direction into Architecture Intent Documents (AID) based on the latest alignment and state intelligence. - Pattern Evolution: Promoting successful, validated implementation heuristics into formal, governed architectural patterns. - Governance Signal Evolution: Transforming continuously validated operational signals into governance participation refinements, capability intelligence, and evolving architectural direction.

docs.title: Capability Intelligence Workflows
docs.text: Operational intelligence is derived from the continuous observation of system evolution: - Capability Extraction: A deterministic process that maps architectural deltas to normalized operational capabilities, ensuring the system remains "aware" of its own implementation trajectory. - Lineage Tracking: Monitoring the evolution of capabilities over time to detect architectural instability or regression. - Signal Interpretation: Transforming implementation evolution, capability lineage, and operational participation changes into continuously actionable governance intelligence feeding back into the operational governance lifecycle.

docs.title: Continuous Validation Alignment
docs.text: Continuous validation serves as the operational trust mechanism connecting all workflows: - Deterministic Validation: Hard-coded structural and rule-based checks that provide the foundation of trust. - Semantic Validation: LLM-augmented reasoning that assesses alignment with higher-level architectural intent and philosophy. - Evidence-Before-Autonomy: Workflows are structured to require deterministic evidence of alignment before granting participants increased implementation autonomy. - Continuous Trust Calibration: Operational trust evolves incrementally through repeated validation, participation quality, and sustained architectural alignment across workflows.

docs.title: Operational Trust Flow
docs.text: Trust evolves operationally through the consistent demonstration of alignment across workflows: - Autonomy Calibration: Governance participation boundaries evolve continuously based on validation evidence, implementation alignment, and operational participation quality. - Escalation Thresholds: Precise points where automated detection hand-offs to human authoritative review (e.g., critical alignment findings or ambiguous steering drift). - Authoritative Human Governance: While workflows are participatory, human architects remain the authoritative source for final decisions and high-impact trade-off resolution.

docs.title: Human and System Participation
docs.text: The mapping clarifies the distinct but intersecting roles of all participants: - Human Architects: Define the rules, approve the patterns, and resolve escalated governance signals. - Coding Agents: Act as active participants that consume Skills and rules to produce aligned implementations. - Orchestration Systems: Governance-aware orchestration coordinates operational participation flows, validation sequencing, and governance intelligence propagation across continuously evolving delivery ecosystems. - Validation Systems: Act as the impartial providers of deterministic evidence required to sustain the trust model.

docs.title: GovernanceOps Integration
docs.text: GovernanceOps operationalizes the evolution of the governance system itself: - Participation Evolution: Continuously refining the interaction models between humans and agents based on operational feedback. - Pattern Promotion: Using intelligence from implementation evolution to identify and formalize new architectural patterns. - Drift Resolution: Systematically addressing misalignment by refining rules or updating architectural intent. - Governance Intelligence Evolution: Continuously refining governance participation models, operational intelligence interpretation, and architectural coherence mechanisms based on real delivery behaviour.

docs.title: Lightweight Governance Principles
docs.text: The workflow mapping adheres to principles of operational sustainability: - Simplicity over Sprawl: Favoring direct participation in Skills and validation over complex, multi-layered orchestration. - Operational Usefulness: Ensuring every workflow step contributes a clear, actionable signal to either prevent, detect, or provide feedback. - Governance through Enablement: Positioning governance as a supportive infrastructure that accelerates delivery by providing clear boundaries and reusable patterns. - Participation over Centralisation: Embedding governance directly into operational participation workflows rather than relying on isolated centralized review structures.

docs.title: Closing Reflection
docs.text: Governance in the Continuous Architecture System is an active operational participation model, not a set of external constraints. By mapping workflows directly to the Prevent ↔ Detect ↔ Feedback loops, CAS ensures that architecture remains a continuously operationalized capability. This integrated operational participation approach establishes the governance infrastructure necessary for sustainable, capability-aware, trust-based software construction in continuously evolving AI-native delivery ecosystems.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text: The Operational Governance Model defines the strategic framework for governing architectural evolution within the Continuous Architecture System (CAS). As delivery ecosystems transition toward AI-native software construction, governance must shift from a centralized, reactive checkpoint model to a distributed, continuous operational capability. This model provides the conceptual substrate for integrating architecture as active operational participation, embedding operational intelligence, continuous validation, architecture participation, and governance-aware agentic workflows into a coherent operational governance infrastructure.

docs.title: The Governance Problem
docs.text:
Traditional governance models are increasingly decoupled from the pace of modern delivery. Key challenges include:

  • Architecture Drift: The widening gap between intended design and implementation reality.
  • Reactive Governance: Controls that trigger too late in the lifecycle, relying on centralized review rather than continuous participation.
  • Operational Visibility Gaps: The inability to derive real-time governance signals from how architectural decisions manifest in execution.
  • Scaling Participation: The difficulty of sustaining architectural coherence, operational trust, and governance participation as delivery evolves into mixed ecosystems of humans, orchestration systems, and coding agents.

docs.title: AI Native Delivery Reality
docs.text:
The emergence of AI-native delivery introduces operational realities that break static governance models:

  • Continuous Change: Higher velocity of code generation and structural refactoring.
  • Agentic Participation: Coding agents acting as primary implementers, requiring deterministic architectural boundaries to function safely.
  • Semantic Reasoning: The use of LLMs to interpret and generate architectural intent, introducing non-deterministic elements into delivery execution.
  • Rapid Evolution: Implementation patterns that evolve faster than passive documentation or decoupled governance boards can capture.
  • Continuous Capability Evolution: Architectural capabilities, operational workflows, and governance expectations evolving continuously alongside implementation activity.

docs.title: Governance Philosophy
docs.text:
The CAS governance philosophy is built on operational realism, enablement, and continuous participation:

  • Governance through Enablement: Providing the operational infrastructure and patterns that make aligned execution the path of least resistance.
  • Lightweight Operational Controls: Minimizing friction while maintaining essential, high-signal guardrails.
  • Continuous Validation: Shifting from point-in-time audits to persistent, participatory architectural monitoring.
  • Architecture Operationalisation: Treating architecture as continuously active operational behaviour embedded directly into execution workflows, rather than static documentation separated from delivery.
  • Operational Trust: Calibrating system autonomy based on deterministic evidence and validated alignment.
  • Operational Participation: Treating governance, architecture, validation, and orchestration as continuously active participants embedded directly within delivery ecosystems.
  • Human Governance Boundaries: Preserving human architects as the ultimate resolvers of semantic ambiguity and high-impact trade-offs.

docs.title: Operational Governance Model
docs.text:
The model operates through continuously evolving participation loops that embed governance directly into delivery:

  • Prevent: Utilizing "Skills" and active constraints to enforce architectural boundaries at the point of creation.
  • Detect: Continuous governance and operational intelligence mechanisms that identify drift between operational intent, capability evolution, and system state.
  • Feedback: Routing derived governance signals back to human architects and coding agents to trigger immediate correction or architectural refinement.
  • Operational Intelligence Loops: Continuously extracting governance signals, implementation evolution insights, and architectural feedback directly from real delivery activity to inform systemic adjustments.
  • Capability Evolution Awareness: Treating implementation evolution, capability lineage, and operational change patterns as governance signals contributing to architectural intelligence.
  • Governance-Aware Orchestration: Integrating governance constraints, lifecycle awareness, and validation checks natively into the execution flows of the delivery ecosystem.

docs.title: Governance Across the Lifecycle
docs.text:
Governance participation evolves according to the operational maturity and lifecycle state of a project. The Prevent → Detect → Feedback model remains constant, but its emphasis changes as understanding, intent, and governance visibility mature.

Lifecycle Phase Governance Focus Project Bootstrap Enable governance participation Baseline Discovery Establish understanding and governance visibility Governance Establishment Define architectural intent and governance context Continuous Governance Maintain alignment through Prevent → Detect → Feedback Rehydration Restore understanding, intent, and governance visibility

docs.title: Project Bootstrap
docs.text:
Governance participation begins with the installation of the governance substrate.

This phase focuses on enabling participation rather than establishing understanding.

Typical capabilities include:

  • Coding-agent integration
  • Governance rules
  • Participation constraints
  • Skills and operational patterns
  • Governance validation infrastructure

docs.title: Baseline Discovery
docs.text:
Discovery establishes a trustworthy operational baseline before governance reasoning begins.

Primary governance responsibilities include:

  • Discovering architectural assets
  • Discovering governance evidence
  • Establishing operational understanding
  • Identifying governance readiness
  • Initializing governance knowledge structures where required

Discovery does not govern.

Discovery understands.

docs.title: Governance Establishment
docs.text:
Governance Establishment transforms discovered understanding into explicit architectural intent and governance participation context.

This phase strengthens governance visibility through:

  • Architecture Intent generation
  • Alignment baselining
  • Governance context creation
  • Initial architectural evolution identification

docs.title: Continuous Governance
docs.text:
Continuous Governance represents the steady-state operational model.

All governance participation loops operate simultaneously:

  • Prevent
  • Detect
  • Feedback

Architecture, governance, validation, orchestration, and coding agents participate continuously through execution-driven operational intelligence loops.

docs.title: Rehydration
docs.text:
Rehydration restores governance visibility when governance state becomes incomplete, unavailable, or requires reconstruction.

Examples include:

  • Repository migration
  • Missing governance artifacts
  • Governance resets
  • Historical reconstruction
  • Recovery from governance state loss

The objective is restoring understanding before resuming normal governance participation.

docs.title: Architecture Participation Model
docs.text:
Architecture and governance function as continuously active operational participation models, shared across human and system actors:

  • Human Architects: Set strategic direction, define operational trust boundaries, resolve complex trade-offs, and interpret high-level governance signals.
  • Architecture Workflows: Continuously operationalize architectural intent through validation, governance feedback, capability evolution awareness, and operational participation loops.
  • Coding Agents: Execute implementations as active participants within governed boundaries, treating architectural patterns as binding operational constraints.
  • Validation Systems: Act as participatory engines that provide the continuous deterministic evidence required to sustain operational trust.
  • Orchestration Participants: Coordinate operational flows, execute governance validation, and sequence actions across continuously evolving delivery systems.

docs.title: Operational Trust Boundaries
docs.text:
Trust within the AI-native delivery ecosystem is dynamically managed through continuous, evidence-based validation:

  • Deterministic vs. Semantic Layers: Relying on deterministic structural validation to bound and verify semantic, agent-driven implementation.
  • Validation before Autonomy: Requiring continuously validated operational alignment before expanding implementation autonomy or shifting governance participation boundaries.
  • Escalation Thresholds: Defining precise operational boundaries where automated governance confidence drops, triggering mandatory human review.
  • Evidence-Based Trust Evolution: Incrementally scaling agentic participation as participants consistently demonstrate architectural coherence and rule adherence.

docs.title: Governance Substrate and Knowledge Workspace
docs.text: CAS distinguishes between two foundational operational capabilities.

docs.title: Governance Substrate
docs.text:
The governance substrate enables participation.

Examples include:

  • Coding-agent integration
  • Governance rules
  • Operational constraints
  • Skills
  • Governance validation workflows
  • Participation guidance

The substrate is established during Project Bootstrap and provides the operational foundation required for governance participation.

docs.title: Governance Knowledge Workspace
docs.text:
The governance knowledge workspace enables persistence, reasoning, and governance intelligence generation.

Examples include:

  • Baseline Assessments
  • Architecture Intent Documents
  • Architecture Evolution Maps
  • Alignment Reports
  • Architecture Work
  • Governance Intelligence
  • Operational Intelligence artifacts

The knowledge workspace is established through Baseline Discovery when required and evolves continuously throughout the lifecycle.

docs.title: Separation of Concerns
docs.text:
The governance substrate enables participation.

The governance knowledge workspace enables understanding.

Maintaining this distinction allows projects to adopt governance participation capabilities independently from governance reasoning and operational intelligence capabilities, supporting lightweight adoption paths while preserving long-term governance evolution.

docs.title: GovernanceOps
docs.text:
GovernanceOps treats governance as a first-class operational infrastructure concern:

  • Governance Lifecycle: Managing the active participation, evolution, and retirement of architectural rules and patterns within the delivery flow.
  • Pattern Evolution: Promoting continuously validated implementation heuristics, participation patterns, and capability evolution signals into formal architectural intelligence.
  • Drift Management: Systematically addressing misalignments through integrated operational intelligence loops rather than out-of-band audits.
  • Signal Promotion: Extracting operational trends from implementation activity and promoting them into actionable governance intelligence.
  • Capability Intelligence: Continuously interpreting capability evolution, implementation change patterns, and operational participation signals to refine governance direction.
  • Participation Evolution: Continuously refining how humans, agents, orchestration systems, validation workflows, and governance lifecycle phases interact operationally.

docs.title: Continuous Validation
docs.text:
Continuous validation is the core participatory engine that connects architectural intent with delivery execution:

  • Structural Validation: Ensuring the physical organization of the codebase complies with actively governed architectural rules.
  • Terminology Governance: Enforcing semantic consistency across ADRs, operational intent, and code execution.
  • Coherence Monitoring: Deriving intelligence on whether new agentic or human changes align with the broader architectural trajectory.
  • Capability Alignment Monitoring: Continuously validating whether evolving implementation capabilities remain aligned with operational governance direction and architectural participation boundaries.
  • Lightweight CI Governance: Embedding high-signal, low-friction architectural validation directly into continuous integration pipelines.

docs.title: Lightweight Governance Principles
docs.text:
To function as sustainable governance infrastructure, CAS resists maximalism and over-engineering:

  • Simplicity over Orchestration Complexity: Prioritizing clear, actionable constraints over opaque, enterprise-heavy orchestration processes.
  • Operational Usefulness over Theoretical Completeness: Ensuring every governance control provides tangible, immediate value to the execution of the delivery process.
  • Participation over Centralization: Embedding governance and architecture directly into operational workflows rather than relying on isolated, external review boards.
  • Sustainable Governance Evolution: Building governance infrastructure that organically adapts to implementation realities without requiring massive, manual recalibration.
  • Understanding Before Governance: Establishing trustworthy operational understanding before governance reasoning or intervention begins.

docs.title: Future Exploration Areas
docs.text:
The model establishes a foundation for advancing governance-aware AI native infrastructure:

  • Narrative Governance: Using AI to continuously generate and maintain the architectural story of an evolving system.
  • Predictive Operational Intelligence: Applying continuously derived signals to detect governance drift and architectural instability before they manifest as systemic debt.
  • Architecture Cognition: Exploring how coding agents can develop an operational "sense" of architectural style, philosophy, and trust boundaries.
  • Governance Cognition: Exploring how governance systems can continuously learn operational participation patterns, architectural coherence signals, and trust evolution heuristics.
  • Enterprise Participation Scaling: Adapting lightweight, participatory governance for complex, multi-system enterprise environments without reverting to centralized control.

docs.title: Closing Reflection
docs.text: Governance in AI-native software construction is not a centralized hurdle to be cleared, but a continuously active operational participation model embedded directly into the system's evolution. By treating architecture as continuously operationalized behaviour rather than static documentation, CAS provides the operational governance infrastructure required to keep AI-native delivery ecosystems sustainable, operationally trustworthy, capability-aware, and continuously aligned with human intent through active architectural participation.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text: The Operational Participation Mapping defines how human and system actors interact within the Continuous Architecture System (CAS) to sustain architectural coherence. In AI-native delivery ecosystems, governance is no longer a centralized mandate but a distributed, participatory behavior. This mapping establishes how participation occurs across workflows, how participants contribute to systemic intelligence, and how operational trust is dynamically calibrated through continuous interaction.

docs.title: Operational Participation Philosophy
docs.text: CAS shifts the governance paradigm from centralized control to distributed participation: - Participation over Centralization: Governance emerges from the active engagement of all delivery actors rather than through external review boards. - Architecture as Active Participation: Architecture is not a static reference but a continuously operationalized behavior shared across the ecosystem. - Continuous Involvement: Participants are engaged throughout the delivery lifecycle—from initial intent to implementation and feedback. - Participation-Aware Governance: The system dynamically adjusts its controls and trust boundaries based on the quality and consistency of participant behavior.

docs.title: Human Architect Participation
docs.text: Human architects participate as the strategic and semantic anchors of the system: - Strategic Direction: Defining the high-level intent, principles, and long-term trajectory of the architecture. - Semantic Interpretation: Resolving architectural ambiguities and complex trade-offs that exceed the reasoning capabilities of automated systems. - Governance Escalation: Acting as the authoritative decision-makers when automated validation or steering signals reach defined escalation thresholds. - Trust Boundary Definition: Establishing the criteria for agentic autonomy and calibrating the sensitivity of detection mechanisms. - Model Evolution: Continuously refining the governance rules, participation models, and patterns based on operational intelligence.

docs.title: Coding Agent Participation
docs.text: Coding agents participate as governed implementers within the delivery flow: - Governed Implementation: Executing technical changes while natively consuming CAS Skills and architectural constraints. - Boundary Participation: Respecting and reinforcing defined module boundaries and architectural patterns during the construction process. - Continuous Validation Interaction: Engaging with automated validation loops to receive and act upon immediate alignment feedback. - Trust Calibration: Providing the implementation evidence required to sustain or expand their operational autonomy. - Pattern Consumption: Treating the architectural patterns catalogue as binding operational guidance for all construction tasks.

docs.title: Validation System Participation
docs.text: Validation systems participate as the impartial providers of deterministic evidence: - Trust Substantiation: Providing the objective data points (tests, structural checks, contract validation) required to maintain operational trust. - Boundary Calibration: Identifying and flagging drift, which triggers the narrowing or shifting of participation boundaries. - Coherence Reinforcement: Continuously asserting architectural rules to ensure that every participant's contribution aligns with the system's structural integrity. - Evidence Generation: Producing the "proof of alignment" that enables participants to move through the delivery lifecycle without manual checkpoints.

docs.title: GovernanceOps Participation
docs.text: GovernanceOps participates by operationalizing the evolution of the governance system: - Signal Promotion: Identifying successful implementation heuristics from real delivery activity and promoting them into formal architectural patterns. - Participation Evolution: Refining how humans and agents interact within workflows to reduce friction and improve alignment quality. - Drift Management: Systematically addressing architectural fragmentation by updating rules or intent based on feedback from the Detect loop. - Intelligence Refinement: Ensuring that governance signals are interpreted correctly and routed to the appropriate participants for action.

docs.title: Orchestration Participation
docs.text: Orchestration systems coordinate the participatory flows across the ecosystem: - Flow Coordination: Sequencing the interactions between agents, validation systems, and human architects to ensure the Prevent ↔ Detect ↔ Feedback loop remains closed. - Intelligence Propagation: Ensuring that governance intent (ADRs, Rules) is correctly distributed to all participants and that feedback signals are captured and persisted. - Governance-Aware Execution: Maintaining the operational environment where participation occurs, ensuring that all workflows are bounded by the current governance context.

docs.title: Operational Intelligence Participation
docs.text: Operational intelligence systems participate by deriving meaning from delivery activity: - Signal Synthesis: Extracting high-level governance signals from raw implementation deltas and validation outcomes. - Participation Awareness: Monitoring how participants engage with the governance model to identify trends, bottlenecks, or systematic drift. - Coherence Monitoring: Providing a real-time view of architectural health by interpreting the cumulative impact of participation across all delivery streams. - Refinement Feedback: Feeding intelligence back into the Prevent loop to refine Skills and constraints.

docs.title: Capability Intelligence Participation
docs.text: Capability-aware systems participate by tracking the evolution of the system's functional landscape: - Implementation Interpretation: Mapping architectural deltas to normalized operational capabilities to maintain an accurate view of "what the system can do." - Lineage Tracking: Monitoring how capabilities evolve, ensuring that implementation changes do not silently degrade architectural integrity over time. - Evolution Visibility: Providing architects with the evidence-based lineage required to make informed decisions about future system trajectory.

docs.title: Participation Trust Boundaries
docs.text: Trust is the dynamic regulator of participation within the ecosystem: - Autonomy Calibration: Participants (especially agents) gain implementation autonomy as they consistently demonstrate alignment through deterministic validation. - Escalation Participation: Automated systems identify their own confidence limits, escalating to human participation when architectural risk exceeds trust thresholds. - Deterministic vs. Semantic Participation: Hard-coded rules provide the "floor" of participation, while semantic reasoning and human review provide the "ceiling" for complex intent. - Evidence-Based Trust: Trust is never assumed; it is continuously earned and verified through active participation in validation workflows.

docs.title: Cross-Participant Participation Flows
docs.text: Governance intelligence flows through the interactions of all participants: - Signal Choreography: A coding agent (Prevent) produces a change that is verified by a validation system (Detect), which generates a signal for an architect (Feedback) if drift occurs. - Intelligence Propagation: Decisions made by architects are instantly operationalized through updated rules and Skills, which then guide the next implementation cycle for agents. - Validation-Driven Trust: Validation outcomes continuously update the trust scores of participants, shifting the boundaries of their future participation.

docs.title: Lightweight Participation Principles
docs.text: To remain sustainable, participation must be frictionless and high-signal: - Enablement over Constraint: Focusing participation on "making things better" rather than "stopping things from happening." - Participation over Review: Favoring active involvement in governed construction over passive, out-of-band review processes. - Operational Usefulness: Ensuring that every participant contribution provides tangible value to either delivery velocity or architectural coherence. - Sustainable Evolution: Building participation models that can scale and adapt without introducing organizational bureaucracy.

docs.title: Closing Reflection
docs.text: Governance in the Continuous Architecture System is achieved through active operational participation. By treating architecture as a shared, continuously active capability, CAS enables a collaborative ecosystem where humans, agents, and automated systems reinforce each other. This integrated participation model ensures that AI-native delivery remains sustainable, trustworthy, and continuously aligned with strategic intent.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
The CAS Project Bootstrap Model defines how governance participation capabilities and governance knowledge capabilities are introduced into a repository before governance reasoning begins.

The model complements the CAS Project Lifecycle Model by describing the responsibilities, outputs, and transitions associated with preparing a repository for Baseline Discovery and ongoing governance participation.

Bootstrap is not responsible for governance reasoning.

Bootstrap is responsible for enabling governance participation and establishing the operational foundations required for governance evolution.

docs.title: Bootstrap Philosophy
docs.text: Bootstrap is guided by five principles:

docs.title: Participation Before Governance
docs.text: Projects should be capable of participating in governance before governance reasoning is attempted.

docs.title: Lightweight Adoption
docs.text: Projects should be able to adopt CAS incrementally without requiring immediate governance maturity.

docs.title: Separation of Concerns
docs.text: Governance participation infrastructure and governance knowledge infrastructure are distinct operational capabilities.

docs.title: Evidence Before Automation
docs.text:
Bootstrap establishes foundations.

Governance intelligence emerges later through Discovery and continuous operational participation.

docs.title: Operational Simplicity
docs.text: Bootstrap should minimise friction while maximising future governance capability.

docs.title: Bootstrap Overview
docs.text:
Bootstrap consists of two complementary operational capabilities:

Governance Substrate Installation
                ↓
Knowledge Workspace Initialization
                ↓
Baseline Discovery

Both capabilities are required before governance understanding can be established reliably.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Enable governance participation within a repository.

This capability provides the operational infrastructure required for coding-agent collaboration, governance validation, architecture participation, and execution-aware governance workflows.

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Install coding-agent integration assets
  • Install governance participation assets
  • Install governance validation infrastructure
  • Establish operational constraints and participation rules
  • Enable governance-aware execution workflows

docs.title: Typical Assets
docs.text:
Examples include:

  • AGENTS.md
  • GEMINI.md
  • CLAUDE.md
  • project rules
  • operational patterns
  • governance validation workflows
  • governance configuration
  • CI governance automation

docs.title: Outputs
docs.text:
Examples:

AGENTS.md
GEMINI.md
CLAUDE.md

.cas/
.github/workflows/

docs.title: Success Criteria
docs.text: The repository can participate in governance-aware delivery workflows.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Establish the governance persistence layer required for operational intelligence, governance visibility, and continuous architectural evolution.

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Create governance knowledge structure
  • Establish governance persistence locations
  • Prepare operational intelligence storage
  • Enable governance artifact generation
  • Establish project-specific governance workspace

docs.title: Typical Structure
docs.text:
Example:

knowledge/
├── global/
│   ├── models/
│   ├── decisions/
│   ├── patterns/
│   └── governance/
│
└── projects/
    └── <project>/
        ├── baseline/
        ├── alignment/
        ├── intent/
        ├── work/
        ├── evolution/
        └── intelligence/

docs.title: Outputs
docs.text:
Examples:

  • governance workspace
  • governance persistence structure
  • operational intelligence workspace
  • project governance folders

docs.title: Success Criteria
docs.text: Governance artifacts can be generated, persisted, and evolved throughout the lifecycle.

docs.title: Relationship Between Substrate and Workspace
docs.text:
The Governance Substrate enables participation.

The Knowledge Workspace enables understanding.

Both are required for effective governance participation.

Governance Substrate
        ↓
Enables Participation

Knowledge Workspace
        ↓
Enables Understanding

This separation supports lightweight adoption while preserving long-term governance capabilities.

docs.title: Discovery Prerequisites
docs.text: Baseline Discovery should only execute when:

docs.title: Governance Substrate Available
docs.text:
Examples:

  • coding-agent integration installed
  • governance rules available
  • governance validation available

docs.title: Knowledge Workspace Available
docs.text:
Examples:

  • governance persistence structure exists
  • project workspace exists
  • governance artifacts can be stored

docs.title: Repository Accessible
docs.text:
Examples:

  • source code available
  • documentation available
  • project assets available

docs.title: Bootstrap Lifecycle Transition
docs.text:
Bootstrap establishes the operational conditions required for governance understanding.

Repository
        ↓
Governance Substrate Installation
        ↓
Knowledge Workspace Initialization
        ↓
Bootstrap Complete
        ↓
Baseline Discovery Eligible

Bootstrap completion does not imply governance understanding.

Bootstrap completion only indicates readiness for Discovery.

docs.title: Bootstrap Creates
docs.text:
• governance substrate
  • governance workspace
  • governance configuration
  • governance structure

docs.title: Discovery Creates
docs.text:
• baseline assessments
  • governance readiness evaluations
  • initial architecture work candidates
  • initial architectural understanding

docs.title: Governance Establishment Creates
docs.text:
• Architecture Intent Documents
  • alignment baselines
  • governance participation context

docs.title: Continuous Governance Creates
docs.text:
• alignment reports
  • architecture evolution maps
  • architecture work
  • governance intelligence
  • operational intelligence

docs.title: Relationship to the Project Lifecycle
docs.text:
The Bootstrap Model supports the first phases of the CAS Project Lifecycle.

Project Bootstrap
        ↓
Baseline Discovery
        ↓
Governance Establishment
        ↓
Continuous Governance
        ↓
Rehydration

Bootstrap focuses exclusively on enabling the first transition.

docs.title: Future Evolution
docs.text:
Potential future areas include:

  • automated workspace generation
  • repository classification
  • bootstrap validation
  • governance-aware repository templates
  • multi-repository bootstrap orchestration
  • enterprise-scale governance initialization

These areas remain exploratory and should evolve only when operational experience justifies additional complexity.

docs.title: Closing Reflection
docs.text:
Governance cannot emerge from an empty repository.

Before governance can reason, align, evolve, or generate intelligence, the operational foundations for participation and understanding must exist.

The Bootstrap Model establishes those foundations while preserving the CAS principles of lightweight governance, operational participation, and execution-driven evolution.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
The CAS Project Lifecycle Model defines how projects enter, establish, participate in, and evolve within the Continuous Architecture System (CAS).

While the Operational Governance Model defines governance philosophy, the Governance Workflow Mapping defines governance flows, and the Operational Participation Mapping defines participant interactions, this model defines the lifecycle through which a project becomes a governed operational ecosystem.

The lifecycle provides a structured path from initial onboarding through continuous governance participation while preserving the CAS principles of lightweight governance, architecture operationalisation, operational intelligence, and execution-driven evolution.

docs.title: Lifecycle Philosophy
docs.text: The lifecycle is guided by several foundational principles:

docs.title: Understand Before Governing
