docs.text: Governance should not reason about a system before establishing a trustworthy understanding of its current state.

docs.title: Participation Before Control
docs.text: Projects enter governance through operational participation rather than mandatory review gates.

docs.title: Evidence Before Autonomy
docs.text: Governance confidence is earned through observable evidence and validated alignment.

docs.title: Execution-Driven Evolution
docs.text: Lifecycle progression is informed by real implementation activity rather than theoretical process completion.

docs.title: Lightweight Operational Governance
docs.text: Governance infrastructure should enable delivery rather than create bureaucratic overhead.

docs.title: Lifecycle Overview
docs.text:
Projects evolve through five operational phases:

Project Bootstrap
        ↓
Baseline Discovery
        ↓
Governance Establishment
        ↓
Continuous Governance
        ↓
Rehydration (when required)

Each phase establishes the operational conditions necessary for the next phase while maintaining alignment with architectural intent.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Introduce CAS governance infrastructure into a repository.

Bootstrap prepares a project for governance participation without requiring prior architectural artifacts.

docs.title: Typical Activities
docs.text:
• Install governance workspace
  • Configure project participation rules
  • Establish governance context
  • Install required operational assets
  • Prepare lifecycle execution environment
  • Install coding-agent integration assets
  • Install governance substrate components
  • Configure operational governance participation

docs.title: Outputs
docs.text:
Examples:

  • AGENTS.md
  • GEMINI.md
  • CLAUDE.md
  • governance configuration
  • project rules
  • onboarding assets
  • participation contracts
  • CI governance validation

docs.title: Bootstrap Scope
docs.text:
Bootstrap enables participation.

Bootstrap does not establish architectural understanding.

Bootstrap does not create governance knowledge artifacts.

Bootstrap prepares the repository to participate in subsequent lifecycle phases.

docs.title: Exit Criteria
docs.text: The project is capable of participating in Baseline Discovery.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Establish a trustworthy understanding of the current repository state.

Discovery identifies what currently exists before governance reasoning begins.

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Discover architectural assets
  • Discover governance evidence
  • Identify operational capabilities
  • Infer architectural intent
  • Establish baseline understanding
  • Identify governance readiness

docs.title: Outputs
docs.text:
Examples:

  • baseline assessment
  • initial architecture evolution map
  • initial architecture work candidates
  • governance readiness indicators

docs.title: Key Principle
docs.text:
Discovery does not govern.

Discovery understands.

docs.title: Exit Criteria
docs.text: The project possesses a validated operational baseline suitable for governance participation.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Transform baseline understanding into explicit architectural intent and governance context.

Baseline Discovery assumes governance participation infrastructure is already available.

Where governance knowledge structures do not yet exist, Discovery is responsible for initializing the minimum knowledge workspace required to establish and persist governance understanding.

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Generate architectural intent
  • Establish alignment baseline
  • Define governance participation context
  • Identify initial architectural evolution opportunities
  • Establish governance knowledge workspace when required

docs.title: Outputs
docs.text:
Examples:

  • Architecture Intent Document (AID)
  • alignment baseline
  • governance participation context
  • initial governance intelligence
  • governance knowledge workspace

docs.title: Exit Criteria
docs.text: Architectural intent is sufficiently explicit to guide ongoing governance participation.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Operate the Prevent → Detect → Feedback governance model continuously throughout project evolution.

docs.title: Prevent
docs.text:
• Skills
  • Rules
  • Constraints
  • Participation guidance

docs.title: Detect
docs.text:
• Alignment analysis
  • Governance drift detection
  • Operational intelligence
  • Capability evolution monitoring

docs.title: Feedback
docs.text:
• Architecture work generation
  • Pattern evolution
  • Governance refinement
  • Intent evolution

docs.title: Outputs
docs.text:
Examples:

  • alignment reports
  • architecture work
  • AEM updates
  • governance intelligence
  • operational participation signals

docs.title: Lifecycle Characteristics
docs.text: Continuous Governance represents the normal operational state of a mature CAS-enabled project.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Reconstruct governance understanding when operational governance state becomes incomplete or unavailable.

docs.title: Example Scenarios
docs.text:
• Missing governance artifacts
  • Repository migration
  • Governance reset
  • Historical reconstruction
  • Operational recovery

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Re-establish baseline understanding
  • Reconstruct intent
  • Rebuild governance visibility
  • Re-enter Continuous Governance

docs.title: Outputs
docs.text:
Examples:

  • refreshed baseline assessment
  • reconstructed AID
  • reconstructed AEM
  • governance recovery recommendations

docs.title: Exit Criteria
docs.text: The project can safely return to Continuous Governance.

docs.title: Governance Participation Across the Lifecycle
docs.text:
The lifecycle does not replace governance.

Instead, governance participation evolves through each phase:

Phase Primary Focus Bootstrap Enable governance participation Discovery Establish understanding and initialize governance knowledge Governance Establishment Define intent Continuous Governance Maintain alignment through Prevent → Detect → Feedback Rehydration Restore understanding and governance visibility

The Prevent → Detect → Feedback model remains active but adapts to the maturity and operational state of the project.

docs.title: Relationship to Existing Governance Models
docs.text:
The Project Lifecycle Model complements the existing CAS governance ecosystem:

Model Primary Question Operational Governance Model Why governance exists Governance Workflow Mapping How governance flows Operational Participation Mapping Who participates Project Lifecycle Model When governance participation occurs

Together these models provide a complete operational view of governance philosophy, participation, workflows, and lifecycle progression.

docs.title: Governance Substrate and Knowledge Workspace
docs.text: CAS distinguishes between two foundational operational capabilities.

docs.title: Governance Substrate
docs.text:
Provides participation capabilities including:

  • coding-agent integration
  • operational rules
  • governance constraints
  • validation workflows
  • participation guidance

The governance substrate is established during Project Bootstrap.

docs.title: Knowledge Workspace
docs.text:
Provides governance persistence and operational intelligence capabilities including:

  • baseline assessments
  • architecture intent
  • architecture evolution maps
  • alignment reports
  • architecture work
  • governance intelligence

The knowledge workspace is established during Baseline Discovery when required.

This separation allows projects to adopt governance participation independently from governance knowledge persistence and operational reasoning capabilities.

docs.title: Future Evolution
docs.text:
Potential future areas include:

  • automated bootstrap assistance
  • governance workspace generation
  • multi-repository onboarding
  • enterprise-scale participation models
  • governance-aware project migration
  • cross-project intelligence propagation

These areas remain exploratory and should only evolve when justified by operational experience.

docs.title: Closing Reflection
docs.text:
Projects do not begin with governance.

Projects begin with understanding.

The CAS Project Lifecycle Model establishes a lightweight operational path from initial onboarding to continuous governance participation, ensuring that architectural intent, operational intelligence, and governance awareness emerge from real delivery activity rather than imposed process structures.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
The CAS Workflow Execution Model defines how a workflow becomes a first-class executable capability within the Continuous Architecture System (CAS).

This model formalises the execution contract already present in CAS Runner and the Knowledge Layer. It does not redesign CAS Runner. It explains how current workflow execution works, where responsibilities sit, and what onboarding conditions future workflows must satisfy before they can be treated as runtime-compatible CAS capabilities.

CAS already contains governance models that explain why governance exists, how projects enter governance, and how workflows participate in Prevent, Detect, and Feedback loops. This model explains the execution boundary beneath those models:

  • CAS Runner orchestrates.
  • Workflow steps prepare, reason, validate, and return governed outputs.
  • The Reasoning Execution Contract mediates semantic reasoning.
  • The Knowledge Provider supplies and persists governed knowledge.
  • Prompts and schemas act as executable workflow contracts.

The model is grounded in the current CAS implementation, including the registered runtime flows, the step execution pattern, prompt discovery, schema validation, provider abstraction, telemetry, and Knowledge Provider I/O boundary.

docs.title: Workflow Philosophy
docs.text:
CAS workflows are operational governance capabilities, not isolated prompts.

A first-class workflow must be executable, bounded, observable, and able to participate in the CAS knowledge lifecycle. It must convert governed inputs into governed outputs without taking ownership of orchestration concerns that belong to the runner.

docs.title: Runner Orchestrates
docs.text:
CAS Runner owns execution sequencing, shared context, provider initialization, configuration resolution, telemetry, evaluation, and persistence coordination.

The runner decides which steps execute and in what order. Workflow steps do not self-schedule or decide the pipeline topology at runtime.

docs.title: Workflow Reasons
docs.text:
Workflow steps own domain transformation. A step prepares the inputs it needs, invokes deterministic logic and semantic reasoning where appropriate, validates its output shape, and returns named outputs to the runner.

Workflow steps should not own global flow orchestration, persistence routing, provider selection, or cross-step scheduling.

docs.title: Knowledge Provider Provides Knowledge
docs.text:
The Knowledge Provider owns the persistence and retrieval boundary for governed knowledge. Workflows consume knowledge through this boundary instead of directly inventing storage rules.

The current local provider loads rules, project vision, architecture work, latest AID, foundational assets, project configuration, and capability lineage. It persists known output types such as rule context, architecture work, AEM, alignment reports, AID, baseline assessments, execution profiles, capability lineage, and evaluations.

docs.title: Prompts Are Contracts
docs.text: Prompt files are not incidental text. In CAS Runner they are loaded through the Prompt Repository as versioned reasoning definitions. Prompt metadata can name schemas and semantic contract versions. The Reasoning Execution Contract injects runtime input context, invokes the provider, and validates structured output where a schema exists.

docs.title: Deterministic Core, Selective Augmentation
docs.text: CAS uses deterministic orchestration and deterministic base computations where possible, with LLM reasoning bounded behind provider and reasoning contracts. Alignment, AEM, AID, discovery, and rule-context steps may use semantic reasoning, but deterministic summaries, fallback paths, schema validation, and explicit output keys preserve execution integrity.

docs.title: Execution Model Overview
docs.text:
At runtime, CAS executes a workflow as a fixed, dependency-ordered pipeline:

Flow request
     |
     v
Resolve registered flow
     |
     v
Initialize execution context
     |
     v
Load project knowledge inputs
     |
     v
For each registered step:
  prepare_inputs(context)
  execute(inputs, reasoning_engine)
  validate(output)
  persist_outputs(output)
  update shared context
     |
     v
Persist telemetry and run evaluation

This implements the orchestrator-managed-pipeline pattern. The runner provides a predictable execution sequence, explicit step handoffs, and traceable data lineage across workflow stages.

The current runtime registry includes:

Flow Current registered steps Execution role orchestration-flow rules-context-loader, architecture-work-to-evolution-map, alignment-engine, architecture-intent-generator Normal governance orchestration loop discovery-flow baseline-discovery, rules-context-loader, architecture-work-to-evolution-map, alignment-engine, architecture-intent-generator Baseline discovery followed by governance state generation steering-projection-flow steering-projection Steering drift and projection workflow

The conversation-to-architecture-work step exists in code and prompt/workflow documentation, but it is not currently active in the registered orchestration flow because it is commented out in the flow registry. It should therefore be treated as implemented but not currently onboarded as an active runtime step in the default flow.

docs.title: Workflow Lifecycle
docs.text: A CAS workflow becomes executable through a lifecycle that moves from governed context to persisted outputs.

docs.title: 1. Context Acquisition
docs.text:
The runner initializes project-level execution context.

Current context includes:

  • project identifier
  • project root
  • workspace root
  • rule context
  • architecture work
  • latest AID
  • project vision
  • patterns
  • project configuration
  • resolved execution profile
  • observability provider

Before step execution, the runner loads current architecture work, latest AID, and project vision from the Knowledge Provider. Individual steps can prepare additional inputs, such as global and project rules or foundational assets.

docs.title: 2. Dependency Resolution
docs.text:
Dependencies are satisfied through explicit step ordering and shared context.

Each step receives the current context and prepares only the input shape it needs. Outputs from prior steps are merged back into context and become available to downstream steps.

Current examples:

  • rules-context-loader loads global and project rules and returns rule_context.
  • architecture-work-to-evolution-map consumes architecture work, baseline assessment, rule context, vision, project, and date.
  • alignment-engine consumes AEM, architecture work, rule context, AID or baseline assessment, project, date, flow name, and prompt base path.
  • architecture-intent-generator consumes AEM, baseline assessment, alignment report, vision, project, and date.
  • baseline-discovery consumes foundational assets and can emit both a baseline assessment and proposed architecture work.

docs.title: 3. Reasoning Execution
docs.text:
Semantic reasoning is invoked through the Reasoning Execution Contract, not directly through arbitrary provider calls.

The contract:

  • identifies the active flow and step
  • loads the latest matching prompt definition
  • supports fallback to shared orchestration prompts
  • parses prompt frontmatter metadata
  • injects runtime input context into the prompt
  • invokes the configured provider
  • loads and applies JSON schemas where available
  • validates structured responses
  • emits telemetry attributes and validation events

This realizes the prompt-as-code-separation, structured-output-via-schema, and llm-provider-abstraction-boundary patterns.

docs.title: 4. Output Validation
docs.text:
Each step validates the output key and the minimum expected structure before the runner persists or propagates results.

Examples:

  • rules-context-loader requires rule_context.
  • architecture-work-to-evolution-map requires aem.frontmatter and aem.sections.
  • alignment-engine requires alignment_report.frontmatter and alignment_report.sections.
  • architecture-intent-generator requires aid.frontmatter and aid.sections.
  • baseline-discovery requires baseline_assessment.

Schema validation also occurs inside the Reasoning Execution Contract when a JSON schema is available for the step.

docs.title: 5. Persistence
docs.text:
The runner delegates persistence to the Knowledge Provider through persist_outputs.

Persistence is output-key driven. The current provider knows how to persist defined artifact types to governed locations in the knowledge workspace. Workflows should therefore emit recognized output keys or require explicit provider support before onboarding.

docs.title: 6. Context Propagation
docs.text:
After persistence, the runner updates shared context with the step output.

List outputs are extended. Non-list outputs are replaced or added by key. This makes step outputs available to later steps without requiring downstream steps to reload the same artifact from disk.

docs.title: 7. Telemetry and Evaluation
docs.text:
Non-dry-run executions persist trace data to the telemetry root and run the evaluation harness against the captured trace.

This makes workflow execution observable and supports future operational intelligence about reasoning quality, provider behavior, cost, and execution reliability.

docs.title: CAS Runner Responsibilities
docs.text:
CAS Runner owns:

  • flow selection
  • registered step sequencing
  • execution context initialization
  • configuration resolution
  • LLM provider initialization
  • Reasoning Execution Contract initialization
  • project knowledge preloading
  • invocation of step lifecycle methods
  • persistence coordination
  • context propagation
  • telemetry persistence
  • orchestration evaluation

The runner should remain deterministic in its core orchestration behavior.

docs.title: Workflow Step Responsibilities
docs.text:
Workflow steps own:

  • declaring a stable step name
  • preparing step-specific inputs from context
  • applying deterministic domain logic where appropriate
  • invoking semantic reasoning through the reasoning contract when needed
  • producing named output keys
  • validating required output structure
  • preserving clear boundaries between deterministic logic and semantic augmentation

Workflow steps should not bypass the Knowledge Provider for governed persistence or directly select LLM providers.

docs.title: Prompt Responsibilities
docs.text:
Prompts own:

  • semantic task framing
  • transformation rules
  • output expectations
  • schema references where applicable
  • semantic contract metadata where applicable

Prompts should not define runner orchestration policy or persistence topology.

docs.title: Schema Responsibilities
docs.text:
Schemas own:

  • machine-readable output contracts
  • validation of structured reasoning responses
  • stability of downstream artifact shape

Any workflow that produces structured knowledge intended for persistence should have an explicit schema or a documented reason why schema validation is not yet available.

docs.title: Knowledge Provider Responsibilities
docs.text:
The Knowledge Provider owns:

  • loading governed inputs from the knowledge workspace
  • resolving the active knowledge workspace root
  • loading project configuration from the governed workspace
  • exposing a stable persistence/retrieval abstraction
  • persisting recognized output types to governed locations
  • preserving backward-compatible functional APIs where current flows still depend on them

The provider boundary allows future storage implementations without changing workflow reasoning semantics.

docs.title: Execution Contract
docs.text: A workflow becomes first-class in CAS Runner when it satisfies the following execution contract.

docs.title: Flow Contract
docs.text:
A flow must have:

  • a stable flow name
  • an ordered list of registered step names
  • a clear lifecycle purpose
  • known upstream trigger conditions
  • known output artifacts
  • a relationship to the project lifecycle or governance participation loop

docs.title: Future Workflow Manifest Alignment
docs.text:
The Flow Contract defines the minimum governance information required for a first-class workflow. A future Workflow Manifest may become the machine-readable representation of this contract.

If introduced, a manifest should describe workflow identity, lifecycle phase, category, inputs, outputs, prompts, schemas, dependencies, and onboarding status without changing the underlying execution architecture.

The manifest should be treated as governance metadata rather than executable orchestration logic. CAS Runner remains responsible for orchestration, while manifests improve governance, discoverability, onboarding, and operational visibility.

docs.title: Step Contract
docs.text:
A step must provide:

  • name
  • prepare_inputs(context)
  • execute(inputs, reasoning_engine)
  • validate(output)

The step name is the join point between:

  • the flow registry
  • prompt discovery
  • schema mapping
  • telemetry naming
  • output validation

docs.title: Prompt Contract
docs.text:
If a step uses reasoning, it must have an available prompt definition.

Prompt discovery currently checks:

  1. latest versioned prompt under the active flow path
  2. unversioned prompt under the active flow path
  3. latest versioned prompt under orchestration-flow as a shared fallback
  4. unversioned prompt under orchestration-flow as a shared fallback

Missing prompts result in an empty reasoning response rather than arbitrary unmanaged behavior.

docs.title: Output Contract
docs.text:
A step output must:

  • be a dictionary
  • use stable top-level output keys
  • satisfy step-level validation
  • satisfy schema validation where a schema applies
  • be persistable by the Knowledge Provider if it represents governed knowledge
  • be safe to merge into shared context for downstream steps

docs.title: Observability Contract
docs.text: Reasoning steps should be traceable through telemetry attributes including provider, model, temperature, flow name, step name, prompt path, prompt version, schema, semantic contract version, token usage, and estimated cost when available.

docs.title: Dependency Contract
docs.text: CAS workflow dependencies are explicit, not hidden.

docs.title: Configuration Dependencies
docs.text:
Runtime configuration must be resolved through cas-config.yaml, environment variables, CLI overrides, and defaults according to the runner configuration loader.

Changes to provider settings or gateway behavior must be reviewed for stability and operational cost impact.

docs.title: Knowledge Dependencies
docs.text:
Knowledge dependencies must be loaded through the Knowledge Provider or through runner-provided context.

Examples:

  • rules
  • architecture work
  • AID
  • vision
  • foundational assets
  • project configuration
  • capability lineage

Workflows should not depend on legacy configuration files or deprecated knowledge paths.

docs.title: Prompt Dependencies
docs.text:
Prompt dependencies must be stored in the configured prompt base path and resolved by the Prompt Repository.

Versioned prompts should be preferred when a workflow is expected to evolve while preserving compatibility.

docs.title: Schema Dependencies
docs.text: Structured workflow outputs should be backed by schemas under the runner schema set or by another explicit schema location referenced by prompt metadata.

docs.title: Step Dependencies
docs.text:
Step dependencies are currently represented by order in the flow registry and by named context keys. A downstream step depends on upstream outputs by reading the corresponding context keys during prepare_inputs.

This is sufficient for the current fixed pipelines. Future dynamic or branching flows would require an explicit dependency graph rather than implicit ordering alone.

docs.title: Workflow Categories
docs.text: CAS workflows can be grouped by their governance role.

docs.title: Discovery Workflows
docs.text:
Discovery workflows establish understanding before governance reasoning proceeds.

Current example:

  • discovery-flow
  • baseline-discovery

These align with the Baseline Discovery phase of the CAS Project Lifecycle Model.

docs.title: Governance Workflows
docs.text:
Governance workflows participate in Prevent, Detect, and Feedback loops by loading rules, evaluating alignment, producing architecture work, and generating governance intelligence.

Current examples:

  • rules-context-loader
  • alignment-engine
  • steering-projection-flow

These align with the Governance Workflow Mapping and Operational Governance Model.

docs.title: Intelligence Workflows
docs.text:
Intelligence workflows synthesize state, intent, drift, and capability signals into higher-level governance understanding.

Current examples:

  • architecture-work-to-evolution-map
  • architecture-intent-generator
  • steering projection generation

These support Continuous Governance by making operational state and intent visible.

docs.title: Ingestion Workflows
docs.text:
Ingestion workflows convert source material into architecture work candidates or governed knowledge inputs.

Current examples:

  • conversation-to-architecture-work exists as a step and prompt/workflow asset but is not active in the current default flow registry.
  • vision-to-architecture-work and discovery-to-architecture-work exist as workflow documentation and represent future or adjacent onboarding candidates.

These workflows should be onboarded carefully because they affect input ownership and architecture work creation.

docs.title: Publication Workflows
docs.text:
Publication workflows prepare governed knowledge for external consumption, dashboards, portals, or delivery surfaces.

Current related surfaces include dashboard, steering projection, and portal publication work. These workflows should preserve the distinction between generating governed knowledge and rendering or publishing it.

docs.title: Workflow Onboarding Process
docs.text: A workflow becomes a first-class CAS capability through the following onboarding process.

docs.title: 1. Establish Workflow Purpose
docs.text:
Define:

  • workflow name
  • lifecycle phase
  • governance participation role
  • category
  • source inputs
  • produced outputs
  • human decision boundaries

The workflow must be tied to a CAS lifecycle phase or governance participation loop.

docs.title: 2. Define Responsibility Boundary
docs.text:
Clarify what belongs to:

  • CAS Runner
  • workflow step
  • prompt
  • schema
  • Knowledge Provider
  • human governance

The workflow must not blur orchestration, reasoning, and persistence responsibilities.

docs.title: 3. Define Input Contract
docs.text:
List all required inputs and identify how each is supplied:

  • runner context
  • Knowledge Provider
  • cas-config.yaml
  • prompt metadata
  • previous step output
  • human-provided source material

Inputs must not be implied through undocumented filesystem assumptions.

docs.title: 4. Define Output Contract
docs.text:
List top-level output keys and governed artifact destinations.

Before runtime onboarding, confirm whether persist_outputs supports each output type. If not, provider support must be added before the workflow is treated as first-class.

docs.title: 5. Add Step Implementation
docs.text:
Create or update a step object with the standard lifecycle:

  • name
  • prepare_inputs
  • execute
  • validate

The step should keep deterministic transformations explicit and call semantic reasoning only through the Reasoning Execution Contract.

docs.title: 6. Add Prompt Contract
docs.text:
Add prompt files under the configured prompt base path using the flow and step naming convention.

Where possible, include frontmatter for:

  • schema
  • semantic contract version
  • prompt version

docs.title: 7. Add Schema Contract
docs.text:
Add or reference JSON schemas for structured outputs.

Schema validation should be part of onboarding for artifacts that downstream steps or persistence logic consume.

docs.title: 8. Register Flow or Step
docs.text:
Register the step in the step registry and add it to the appropriate flow order.

If a step is implemented but intentionally inactive, document that status explicitly. Implemented but unregistered steps are not first-class runtime workflow capabilities.

docs.title: 9. Validate Execution
docs.text:
Validation should cover:

  • smallest relevant step tests
  • flow-level runner behavior
  • schema validation
  • persistence behavior
  • telemetry behavior where reasoning is used
  • dry-run behavior if supported

docs.title: 10. Update Governed Knowledge
docs.text: Update affected models, workflow mappings, diagrams, prompts, rules, and steering guidance where the onboarding changes platform behavior.

docs.title: Current Gaps and Recommendations
docs.text: The current implementation already establishes the core execution model, but several gaps should be made explicit.

docs.title: Existing Behavior
docs.text:
• The runner uses a fixed flow registry.
  • Steps expose a consistent lifecycle shape.
  • Prompt discovery is convention-based.
  • Reasoning is provider-abstracted and schema-aware.
  • Persistence is centralized through the Knowledge Provider.
  • Telemetry and evaluation are part of non-dry-run execution.
  • Some workflows exist as documentation or code assets without being active runtime capabilities.

docs.title: Gaps
docs.text:
• Flow dependencies are implicit in ordered lists rather than described as a declarative dependency graph.
  • Step output keys are validated locally but not governed by a unified workflow contract registry.
  • Persistence support is output-key based and must be manually aligned with new workflow artifacts.
  • Prompt schema mappings are partly hard-coded by step name and partly available through prompt metadata.
  • Implemented but inactive steps can be mistaken for onboarded runtime capabilities.
  • The workflow category model is conceptual and not yet represented in runtime metadata.

docs.title: Recommendations
docs.text:
• Introduce a Workflow Manifest pattern when the number of runtime workflows makes implicit registry entries difficult to govern.

A Workflow Manifest would provide a machine-readable representation of workflow metadata while preserving the current execution model.

Conceptual example:

workflow:
  name: conversation-to-architecture-work
  category: ingestion
  lifecycle_phase: baseline_discovery

inputs:
  - conversations

outputs:
  - architecture_work

prompts:
  - conversation-to-architecture-work.v1.md

schemas:
  - architecture-work.schema.json
  • Require each newly onboarded workflow to document input keys, output keys, persistence destinations, prompt path, schema path, and lifecycle phase.
  • Prefer prompt frontmatter schema references over hard-coded schema mappings for new workflows.
  • Make inactive or experimental steps explicit in registry documentation.
  • Keep orchestration deterministic unless a future workflow has a documented need for branching or dynamic dependency resolution.

These are recommendations for future consistency, not prerequisites for the current runner to remain valid.

docs.title: Relationship to the CAS Project Lifecycle Model
docs.text:
The Project Lifecycle Model explains how projects move through Bootstrap, Baseline Discovery, Governance Establishment, Continuous Governance, and Rehydration.

The Workflow Execution Model explains how executable workflows support those phases.

Examples:

  • Bootstrap prepares the substrate and workspace required for execution.
  • Discovery workflows establish baseline understanding.
  • Governance workflows support Governance Establishment and Continuous Governance.
  • Rehydration workflows would restore executable understanding when knowledge state becomes incomplete.

docs.title: Relationship to the CAS Operational Governance Model
docs.text:
The Operational Governance Model explains the strategic governance philosophy of Prevent, Detect, Feedback, operational intelligence, trust boundaries, and human governance.

The Workflow Execution Model explains how those governance loops become executable through runner-managed pipelines, workflow steps, prompts, schemas, and knowledge persistence.

docs.title: Relationship to the CAS Governance Workflow Mapping
docs.text:
The Governance Workflow Mapping explains how workflows participate in governance loops.

The Workflow Execution Model defines the runtime contract required for those workflows to execute consistently inside CAS Runner.

The mapping answers where a workflow participates. This model answers how a workflow is onboarded and executed.

docs.title: Relationship to the CAS Project Bootstrap Model
docs.text:
The Bootstrap Model distinguishes the Governance Substrate from the Knowledge Workspace.

The Workflow Execution Model depends on both:

  • the substrate provides agent instructions, governance rules, configuration, validation, and execution participation assets
  • the workspace provides the persisted knowledge that workflows load and update

Bootstrap enables participation. Workflow execution operationalizes participation.

docs.title: Relationship to the Knowledge Provider Model
docs.text:
The Knowledge Provider boundary is the execution model's persistence and retrieval contract.

The runner and steps should treat governed knowledge as provider-supplied, not as arbitrary filesystem state. This supports local filesystem execution today while preserving the possibility of future provider implementations.

docs.title: Separation of Concerns
docs.text: Orchestration, reasoning, persistence, prompt definition, schema validation, and human governance are distinct responsibilities.

docs.title: Knowledge First
docs.text: Workflows operate against governed knowledge inputs and persist governed outputs. Execution should strengthen the Knowledge Layer rather than create untracked side effects.

docs.title: Runtime Orchestration
docs.text: CAS workflows are executed by a deterministic runtime pipeline. The runner coordinates; workflow steps transform.

docs.title: Governance by Design
docs.text: Rules, prompts, schemas, provider boundaries, and persistence destinations are part of the workflow contract, not after-the-fact checks.

docs.title: Continuous Governance
docs.text: Every workflow execution can contribute to updated state, alignment findings, architecture work, intent, telemetry, or operational intelligence.

docs.title: Contract-First Architecture
docs.text: New workflows should become first-class through explicit contracts for inputs, outputs, prompts, schemas, dependencies, and persistence.

docs.title: Platform Contract Summary
docs.text:
A CAS workflow is first-class when:

  • it has a registered runtime identity
  • it participates in a known lifecycle or governance loop
  • it executes through CAS Runner or an equivalent governed runner surface
  • its steps implement the standard step lifecycle
  • its prompts are discoverable through the Prompt Repository
  • its semantic reasoning goes through the Reasoning Execution Contract
  • its structured outputs are validated
  • its governed outputs can be persisted through the Knowledge Provider
  • its dependencies are explicit
  • its execution is observable
  • its human governance boundaries are clear

This model establishes the authoritative execution contract for onboarding future workflows into CAS Runner while preserving the current architecture: runner-managed orchestration, workflow-owned reasoning, provider-owned knowledge, and governance-aware persistence.

docs.title: Documents
docs.text:
• cas-coding-agent-collaboration
  • cas-flows-overview
  • cas-governance-actions
  • cas-governance-compliance
  • cas-governance-foundation
  • cas

docs.title: The Emerging Challenge
docs.text:
Coding agents are rapidly transforming how software is created. By automating implementation tasks, accelerating delivery cycles, and reducing operational friction, these systems significantly increase engineering throughput. However, most organisations are integrating coding agents into delivery environments without equivalent evolution in governance mechanisms.

The result is a dangerous asymmetry: software construction accelerates while architectural governance remains largely manual, reactive, and disconnected from implementation reality.

Without operational governance boundaries, coding agents can unintentionally amplify:

  • architectural drift
  • implementation inconsistency
  • undocumented operational behaviour
  • governance bypass
  • repository entropy
  • uncontrolled technology proliferation

The core issue is not that coding agents are unreliable. The issue is that modern delivery systems increasingly allow implementation decisions to occur outside architecture aware operational constraints.

docs.title: Why Prompt-Driven Governance Fails
docs.text:
Most current approaches to coding agent governance rely heavily on:

  • prompt engineering
  • isolated instruction files
  • static documentation
  • human review checkpoints
  • conversational memory

These mechanisms are insufficient for scalable governance because they remain fundamentally non-operational. Prompts and instructions alone cannot guarantee that architectural intent continuously participates in the software construction lifecycle.

As repositories evolve, prompts become fragmented, operational expectations diverge across teams, and governance consistency deteriorates. The problem compounds further in multi agent environments where different tools interpret guidance differently and operate with varying behavioural assumptions.

Traditional governance mechanisms also operate too late. By the time architecture reviews or manual validations occur, architectural entropy has already entered the repository.

docs.title: The CAS Insight
docs.text:
The critical insight behind CAS is that coding agents should not operate autonomously outside governance boundaries. Instead, architecture intent must become an active participant in software construction itself.

CAS treats coding agent collaboration as an operational governance problem rather than merely an automation problem.

This shifts governance from:

  • reactive review
  • post-implementation correction
  • documentation reconciliation

toward:

  • preventive operational guidance
  • governed implementation workflows
  • governed architectural boundaries
  • continuous operational validation

In this model, coding agents do not simply consume prompts. They operate inside governance aware software construction workflows that continuously reconcile repository evolution against architectural intent.

docs.title: The CAS Approach
docs.text: CAS operationalises governance aware coding agent collaboration through a layered governance model that combines preventive and corrective operational controls.

docs.title: Preventive Governance
docs.text:
Preventive governance mechanisms establish architectural boundaries before implementation divergence occurs.

These mechanisms include:

  • governance aware onboarding packages
  • operational guidance contracts (AGENTS.md, CLAUDE.md, GEMINI.md)
  • CAS Skills
  • specification-driven development workflows
  • mandatory architectural sequencing
  • governance aware CI validation
  • operational workflow enforcement

This allows architecture intent to participate directly in the implementation lifecycle from the moment software construction begins.

docs.title: Corrective Governance
docs.text:
Corrective governance mechanisms continuously monitor repository evolution to detect architectural drift that still emerges during delivery.

These workflows include:

  • auditable capability extraction
  • governance drift analysis
  • steering projection workflows
  • operational capability lineage
  • evaluation and validation workflows
  • architecture aware operational reconciliation

Together, these capabilities create continuous governance feedback loops capable of scaling alongside highly automated delivery systems.

docs.title: Auditable Governance Boundaries
docs.text:
A foundational CAS principle is that governance authority must remain reproducible, auditable, and operationally trustworthy.

CAS intentionally separates:

  • auditable governance truth from:
  • semantic operational interpretation

Repository evolution, architectural capability extraction, governance lineage, and operational boundaries remain reproducible and replayable. Semantic reasoning augments governance interpretation but never replaces authoritative governance evidence.

This distinction is critical for ensuring:

  • governance trust
  • operational safety
  • auditability
  • CI/CD enforcement
  • reproducibility
  • scalable enterprise adoption

Rather than creating autonomous governance systems, CAS establishes governance aware operational infrastructure that safely constrains automation inside architecture aligned delivery boundaries.

docs.title: Operational Outcomes
docs.text:
By embedding governance directly into coding agent workflows, CAS transforms software construction from an architecture-blind acceleration mechanism into a governance aware operational system.

The result is:

  • reduced architectural entropy
  • improved implementation consistency
  • scalable coding agent collaboration
  • lower governance friction
  • safer delivery acceleration
  • architecture aware automation
  • continuously aligned repository evolution

Most importantly, CAS enables organisations to scale coding agent adoption without sacrificing architectural coherence, governance integrity, or operational trust.

docs.title: Broader Implications
docs.text:
Governance aware coding agent collaboration represents a necessary evolution in enterprise software delivery. As coding agents increasingly participate in implementation workflows, governance can no longer remain external to the software construction lifecycle.

CAS demonstrates that architecture governance can evolve from passive oversight into an active operational capability that continuously shapes how software is created.

By combining governed operational foundations with governance aware automation, CAS establishes the foundation for scalable, architecture aligned software construction in increasingly automated engineering ecosystems.

The long-term implication is not simply better documentation or safer automation. It is the emergence of governed software construction systems where architecture intent continuously participates in delivery itself.

docs.title: Closing Reflection
docs.text:
Coding agents will fundamentally reshape software engineering. The critical challenge is not whether organisations adopt these systems, but whether governance evolves quickly enough to participate meaningfully in increasingly automated delivery environments.

CAS proposes that the future of architecture governance lies not in restricting automation, but in embedding auditable governance intelligence directly into software construction workflows themselves.

When architecture becomes an active operational participant rather than a passive review function, automation and governance no longer compete. They reinforce each other continuously.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Continuous Architecture System (CAS) operates through structured, goal-oriented orchestration flows. Each flow represents a specific architectural or governance objective, executed via the `cas` CLI. This document provides a comprehensive reference for each flow, its purpose, and how to execute it.

docs.title: 1. Orchestration Flow (`orchestration-flow`)
docs.text:
The primary pipeline for the CAS lifecycle. It transforms governance rules and architecture work into a system-wide evolution map and updated architectural intent.

  • Objective: Synchronize Intended Architecture with Actual Architecture.
  • Key Steps: Rules Loader → AEM Generator → Alignment Engine → AID Generator.
  • CLI Execution: ```bash uv run cas run orchestration-flow --project --provider ```

docs.title: 2. Governance Validator (`governance-validator`)
docs.text:
Automates the structural and integrity checks of the knowledge layer. This flow acts as the primary quality gate in CI/CD.

  • Objective: Ensure structural validity, traceability, and lifecycle compliance.
  • Key Steps: Artifact Discovery → Schema Validation → Traceability Check → Lifecycle Check → Metrics Calculation.
  • CLI Execution: ```bash uv run cas run governance-validator --project ``` (Note: Can also be run via `cas governance validate`)

docs.title: 3. Baseline Discovery (`discovery-flow`)
docs.text:
Forensically maps the initial state of an existing project. This is the first flow executed when onboarding a new repository.

  • Objective: Establish the "Actual State" baseline.
  • Key Steps: Baseline Discovery → Rules Loader → Conversation to AW → AEM Generator → Alignment Engine → AID Generator.
  • CLI Execution: ```bash uv run cas run discovery-flow --project --provider ```

docs.title: 4. Steering Projection (`steering-projection-flow`)
docs.text:
Generates actionable steering proposals to resolve architectural drift identified by the alignment engine.

  • Objective: Propose corrections to keep the codebase aligned with architectural intent.
  • Key Steps: Steering Projection Generation.
  • CLI Execution: ```bash uv run cas run steering-projection-flow --project --provider ```

docs.title: 5. Signal to Pattern (`signal-to-pattern-proposals`)
docs.text:
Analyzes architectural signals and learnings to identify emerging concepts.

  • Objective: Discover new candidate patterns from operational evidence.
  • CLI Execution: ```bash uv run cas run signal-to-pattern-proposals --project --provider ```

docs.title: 6. Pattern Scope Classifier (`pattern-scope-classifier`)
docs.text:
Determines the architectural scope (Global vs. Project) of proposed patterns.

  • Objective: Assign appropriate governance boundaries to emerging concepts.
  • CLI Execution: ```bash uv run cas run pattern-scope-classifier --project --provider ```

docs.title: 7. Pattern Promotion (`pattern-promotion-workflow`)
docs.text:
Evaluates scoped proposals against promotion criteria to generate formal recommendations.

  • Objective: Filter emerging concepts into "Recommended" candidates for institutionalization.
  • CLI Execution: ```bash uv run cas run pattern-promotion-workflow --project --provider ```

docs.title: 8. Promotion Decision Capture (`promotion-decision-capture`)
docs.text:
Captures the human architect's decision on a system-recommended pattern promotion.

  • Objective: Provide the formal human-in-the-loop gate for pattern adoption.
  • CLI Execution: ```bash uv run cas run promotion-decision-capture --project --provider ```

docs.title: 9. Promotion to ADR (`promotion-to-decision-records`)
docs.text:
Transforms accepted promotion decisions into formal Architecture Decision Records (ADRs).

  • Objective: Formalize architectural decisions into the Single Source of Truth.
  • CLI Execution: ```bash uv run cas run promotion-to-decision-records --project --provider ```

docs.title: 10. Approved ADR to Pattern (`approved-adr-to-pattern`)
docs.text:
Converts forum-approved ADRs into institutionalised Pattern artifacts.

  • Objective: Complete the lifecycle by establishing new architectural standards.
  • CLI Execution: ```bash uv run cas run approved-adr-to-pattern --project --provider ```

docs.title: Operational Summary
docs.text:
• Idempotency: All CAS flows are designed to be idempotent; running them multiple times against the same input will yield consistent, versioned artifacts.
  • Human-in-the-Loop: While flows automate the "Detect" and "Propose" stages, the "Decide" stage (Action Framework) remains an explicit human-driven gate.
  • Telemetry: Every execution generates a trace log in `knowledge/runtime/traces/`, enabling continuous evaluation of orchestration quality.

docs.title: The Separation of State and Action
docs.text:
In the early stages of CAS, governance state transitions (like approving an ADR) required manually editing the YAML frontmatter of the artifact file. While functionally correct, this approach conflated the artifact's State with the Action of changing it, leading to operational friction as governance volume grew.

The Governance Action Framework solves this by enforcing a strict architectural principle: Artifacts store state. Actions change state. The Engine governs transitions.

docs.title: The Action Engine
docs.text:
The GovernanceActionEngine is the centralized authority for all governance state changes. It operates on a Transition Registry that defines valid state machines for each artifact type.

For example, a Promotion Decision can transition from pending to accepted, rejected, or deferred. An attempt to transition directly from deferred to accepted will be rejected by the engine.

This engine is exposed through the CAS CLI: 

cas governance approve <artifact-id>
cas governance reject <artifact-id> --rationale "Missing evidence"

docs.title: Immutable Audit Trails
docs.text:
Accountability requires traceability. Every successful state transition executed by the engine generates a formal GovernanceAction audit record.

These records are immutable YAML files stored in knowledge/global/governance/actions/. They capture: - The exact transition (e.g., pending -> accepted) - The target artifact - The executing user identity - The cryptographic timestamp - Any provided rationale

docs.title: Future-Proofing
docs.text: By routing all state changes through a single, registry-driven engine, CAS is prepared for future capabilities such as UI-based "one-click" approvals in the Governance Portal or autonomous AI-driven governance decisions. All interfaces will delegate to the Action Engine, guaranteeing consistent validation and auditing.

docs.title: Why Compliance Exists
docs.text: The Continuous Architecture System (CAS) ensures that architecture evolves with integrity. While Governance Integrity (Sprint 1) focuses on structural validity and Governance Intelligence (Sprint 2) provides visibility, Governance Compliance establishes accountability. It ensures that the governance rules defined by the collective are actively followed and that deviations are explicitly managed.

docs.title: How Compliance Works
docs.text: Compliance in CAS is metadata-driven. We don't hardcode rigid policies in Python; instead, we treat our Governance Rules as the authoritative source of truth for both human guidance and automated evaluation.

docs.title: Rule-Based Model
docs.text:
A rule participates in compliance evaluation if it contains the following frontmatter:

compliance:
  enabled: true
  validation_type: "status_check"
  target: "adrs"
  field: "governance.forum_status"
  expected: "accepted"
  severity: "error"
  recommendation: "Submit for forum review"

The Compliance Engine discovers these enabled rules and evaluates them against the knowledge layer during every validation run.

docs.title: Exception Model
docs.text:
We recognize that architecture is often about managing technical debt and navigating pragmatism. The Compliance Exception model allows architects to explicitly accept a non-compliant state for a specific period.

Exceptions are formal governance artifacts: - Location: knowledge/global/governance/exceptions/ - Governance: Requires rationale, approver, and an expiry date.

Active exceptions suppress CI/CD failures, ensuring the pipeline remains green for accepted debt while keeping that debt highly visible in compliance dashboards.

docs.title: Compliance Lifecycle
docs.text:
1. Rule Definition: A governance rule is established or updated with compliance metadata.
  2. Evaluation: The Compliance Engine runs (locally or in CI/CD).
  3. Detection: Violations are identified and categorized (error, warning, info).
  4. Action:
    • Green: Compliant.
    • Block: Violations (errors) without exceptions fail the build.
    • Exception: Architect records a formal exception to suppress the build.
    • Resolution: Artifact is updated to meet the rule, and the exception is retired.

docs.title: Accountability, Not Bottlenecks
docs.text: The goal of Governance Compliance is to identify gaps early. It provides the data necessary for the Governance Forum to make informed decisions about where to invest in alignment and where to grant flexibility, ensuring that the system's "Actual Architecture" stays aligned with its "Intended Architecture."

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This document explains the governance foundation of the Continuous Architecture System (CAS).

It describes why governance exists, the capabilities currently implemented, and how governance artifacts evolve from architectural signals into institutionalized patterns.

This is a platform narrative rather than an implementation specification.

docs.title: Why Governance Exists
docs.text:
Traditional architecture governance is often periodic, manual, and disconnected from delivery.

Architecture reviews happen after decisions have already been made, creating delays, inconsistent decision-making, and limited organizational learning.

CAS takes a different approach.

Governance is treated as a continuous capability embedded into the architecture lifecycle.

Rather than governing only completed solutions, CAS governs the evolution of architectural knowledge itself.

The objective is to continuously transform:

text Signals ↓ Insights ↓ Patterns ↓ Institutional Knowledge

while preserving traceability, evidence, and architectural intent.

docs.title: Governance Principles
docs.text: The governance foundation is built upon the following principles.

docs.title: Governance as a Continuous Capability
docs.text:
Governance is not a stage.

Governance is an ongoing activity operating throughout the architecture lifecycle.

Architectural learning continuously feeds governance decisions.

Governance decisions continuously influence future architectural work.

docs.title: Traceability First
docs.text:
Every governance artifact must be traceable.

Governance decisions should never appear without supporting evidence and lineage.

CAS therefore maintains explicit relationships between:

text Pattern Proposal ↓ Promotion Recommendation ↓ Promotion Decision ↓ Architecture Decision Record ↓ Institutionalized Pattern

This lineage forms the governance chain.

docs.title: Human Decision Authority
docs.text:
CAS supports governance decisions but does not replace them.

The platform may:

  • identify patterns
  • recommend promotion
  • generate governance artifacts
  • validate governance integrity

The platform does not:

  • approve patterns
  • reject patterns
  • override governance decisions

Architectural authority remains with architects and governance forums.

The governing principle is:

text System validates process. Architect makes decisions.

docs.title: Governance as Code
docs.text:
Governance should be executable.

Governance artifacts are represented as structured knowledge objects.

Governance workflows generate governance artifacts.

Governance validation is automated through CI/CD.

This enables governance to become observable, repeatable, and enforceable.

docs.title: Governance Lifecycle
docs.text:
The governance lifecycle institutionalizes architectural learning.

text Pattern Proposal ↓ Promotion Recommendation ↓ Promotion Decision ↓ Architecture Decision Record (ADR) ↓ Governance Forum Review ↓ Institutionalized Pattern

Each stage produces explicit governance artifacts.

docs.title: Pattern Proposal
docs.text:
Pattern proposals capture recurring architectural observations.

They represent candidate patterns that may become institutional knowledge.

A proposal does not imply approval.

A proposal indicates:

text This observation appears significant enough to warrant governance review.

docs.title: Promotion Recommendation
docs.text:
Promotion recommendations assess whether a proposed pattern should progress toward institutionalization.

Recommendations evaluate:

  • significance
  • recurrence
  • reusability
  • evidence quality
  • strategic alignment

Recommendations support decision making.

They do not make decisions.

docs.title: Promotion Decision
docs.text:
Promotion decisions capture architectural intent.

The responsible architect records whether the pattern should:

text Promote Defer Reject

A promotion decision creates governance accountability.

docs.title: Architecture Decision Record (ADR)
docs.text:
An ADR formalizes governance intent.

The ADR captures:

  • decision
  • rationale
  • context
  • consequences
  • governance traceability

The ADR becomes the primary governance artifact reviewed by the Architecture Forum.

docs.title: Governance Forum Review
docs.text:
Governance Forums review ADRs.

Forums may:

text Approve Reject Approve with Conditions Defer

The review outcome is recorded directly within the ADR.

Governance review represents the formal institutionalization gate.

docs.title: Institutionalized Pattern
docs.text:
Approved ADRs become institutionalized patterns.

Patterns represent reusable architectural knowledge.

Patterns are no longer project-specific observations.

They become part of the CAS knowledge base.

Institutionalized patterns influence:

  • future architecture work
  • onboarding workflows
  • governance validation
  • alignment reviews

docs.title: Governance Artifacts
docs.text: The governance foundation introduces several artifact types.

docs.title: Pattern Proposal
docs.text: Captures a candidate architectural pattern.

docs.title: Promotion Recommendation
docs.text: Captures evidence-based promotion guidance.

docs.title: Promotion Decision
docs.text: Captures architect intent regarding promotion.

docs.title: Architecture Decision Record
docs.text: Captures governance rationale and forum review outcomes.

docs.title: Pattern
docs.text: Captures institutionalized architectural knowledge.

docs.title: Governance Portal
docs.text:
The Governance Portal provides operational visibility of governance state.

The Portal is a projection layer.

It visualizes governance artifacts without becoming the source of truth.

The Portal exposes:

text Pattern Proposals Promotion Recommendations Promotion Decisions ADRs Pending Review Approved ADRs Institutionalized Patterns

The Portal enables architects to navigate governance lineage and monitor governance health.

docs.title: Governance CI/CD
docs.text:
Governance validation is automated.

The Governance Validator continuously verifies:

text Schema Integrity Traceability Integrity Lifecycle Integrity Governance State Integrity

Validation occurs both locally and within CI/CD pipelines.

Governance automation validates process integrity.

Governance automation does not replace architectural judgement.

docs.title: Current Governance Capability Model
docs.text: The following governance capabilities are implemented.

docs.title: Governance Lifecycle
docs.text: Implemented.

docs.title: Governance Artifacts
docs.text: Implemented.

docs.title: Governance Traceability
docs.text: Implemented.

docs.title: Governance Portal
docs.text: Implemented.

docs.title: Governance CI/CD
docs.text: Implemented.

docs.title: Future Governance Evolution
docs.text:
The governance foundation establishes the basis for future governance capabilities.

Planned areas include:

docs.title: Governance Intelligence
docs.text:
Provide operational governance insights, and relies on"

  • Governance Metrics
  • Artifact Reference Resolution
  • Governance Health Reports

Examples:

  • governance health
  • governance trends
  • governance workload visibility
  • governance reporting

docs.title: Governance Compliance
docs.text:
Provide automated compliance validation.

Examples:

  • pattern compliance
  • governance policy validation
  • architectural conformance checks

docs.title: Governance Drift Detection
docs.text:
Identify divergence between institutionalized patterns and implementation reality.

Examples:

  • pattern adoption monitoring
  • architecture drift detection
  • governance risk indicators

docs.title: Architectural Outcome
docs.text:
The governance foundation transforms governance from a periodic review activity into a continuous architectural capability.

CAS continuously converts architectural learning into institutional knowledge while preserving evidence, traceability, and human decision authority.

The resulting governance model combines:

text Governance Generation + Governance Visibility + Governance Validation

to create an operational governance capability embedded within the architecture lifecycle.

docs.title: The Problem
docs.text: Traditional enterprise architecture relies heavily on static documentation to govern complex systems. While these artifacts capture initial strategic intent, they are inherently disconnected from the engineering reality. As systems evolve, a critical gap forms between the documented architecture and the actual implementation, leading to severe documentation drift and a loss of alignment. The fundamental problem is that static governance mechanisms cannot scale to meet the demands of modern delivery velocity without introducing unacceptable friction.

docs.title: Why Traditional Governance Breaks
docs.text: Architecture governance typically degrades not because of engineering failure, but due to systemic operational limitations. Manual review processes and point-in-time assessments are fundamentally incompatible with continuous delivery and agile execution. As an organisation scales, the administrative burden of maintaining architectural alignment across disconnected teams becomes unsustainable. This friction inevitably leads to architectural drift, where the system's operational reality diverges from strategic intent, creating hidden technical debt and operational risk.

docs.title: Shift-Left Governance
docs.text:
Traditional governance mechanisms operate too late in the delivery lifecycle, relying on post-implementation reviews, documentation reconciliation, and periodic oversight to detect architectural divergence. CAS shifts architecture governance directly into the software construction process itself, establishing preventive operational guardrails that guide implementation from the moment code is created while continuously detecting and correcting what still escapes those boundaries.

This creates a dual-governance operational model:

  • Preventive Governance — Governance aware onboarding, CAS Skills, coding agent operational guidance, specification-driven development workflows, and CI validation establish architectural boundaries before implementation diverges.
  • Corrective Governance — Continuous governance workflows, capability extraction, steering projection, evaluation, and operational reconciliation detect and correct architectural drift that still emerges during system evolution.

By embedding governance directly into software construction workflows, CAS transforms architecture from a passive review function into an active operational capability that continuously shapes repository evolution in real time. This governance aware software construction model becomes increasingly critical in environments where coding agents actively participate in delivery workflows.

docs.title: The Architectural Insight
docs.text: The resolution to this drift lies in shifting the paradigm from static documentation and reactive governance toward operational intelligence embedded directly into the software construction lifecycle. The core insight behind the Continuous Architecture System (CAS) is that architecture must operate as a living system—where intent is continuously reconciled against the repository state. By treating Architecture-as-Code, governance evolves from a periodic, manual review process into a continuous, reproducible operational feedback loop that guarantees alignment while preserving auditability, replayability, and governance trust.

docs.title: The CAS Approach
docs.text: CAS operationalises architecture governance by establishing a governance aware operational intelligence system capable of continuously reconciling operational reality against architectural intent. At its core, it relies on auditable capability extraction—analysing repository evolution to derive normalised operational capabilities without relying on stochastic governance decisions. Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) serve as the single, machine-readable source of truth, ensuring that governance remains reproducible, auditable, and operationally trustworthy. CAS also establishes governance aware operational workflows for both engineering teams and coding agents, enabling architecture intent to participate directly in the software construction process itself. This operational model establishes the foundation for governance aware coding agent collaboration, where governed operational foundations participate directly in automated software construction workflows. Through CAS Skills, specification-driven development workflows, operational onboarding contracts, and governance aware CI validation, the system prevents significant categories of architectural drift before they materialise while still continuously detecting and correcting what escapes those preventive boundaries. Through continuous operational feedback loops, the system automatically detects structural anomalies, governance drift, and operational capability evolution, empowering architects to manage governance through enablement and automation rather than manual constraint.

docs.title: Operational Outcomes
docs.text: The implementation of CAS fundamentally reduces architectural drift by combining preventive governance mechanisms with continuous operational visibility into the system's structural integrity. This approach increases governance scalability, allowing small architecture teams to oversee massive transformation initiatives safely. By establishing reproducible feedback loops and mandatory architectural guardrails, CAS improves the alignment between executive strategy, engineering delivery, and coding agent execution, ensuring that continuous transformation remains operationally sustainable, governance aware, and architecturally coherent.

docs.title: Broader Implications
docs.text: CAS represents a necessary maturity evolution in how enterprise architecture operates. It signals the transition from architecture as a passive recording function toward architecture as a continuous operational governance system. This operational governance integration demonstrates that complex governance can be operationalised through reproducible intelligence, continuous feedback loops, and governance aware automation without sacrificing strategic optionality or operational safety. By combining governed operational foundations with automated operational interpretation, CAS establishes the foundation for scalable Governance-as-Code in increasingly automated engineering ecosystems. Ultimately, it provides the operational infrastructure required for sustainable enterprise transformation in an era of growing technological and organisational complexity.

docs.title: Related Narrative
docs.text: The operational implications of governance aware software construction become even more significant in highly automated delivery environments where coding agents actively participate in implementation workflows. The companion narrative Governance Aware Coding Agent Collaboration expands on how CAS establishes governed operational foundations, preventive operational controls, and continuous governance reconciliation mechanisms for scalable coding agent ecosystems.

docs.title: Closing Reflection
docs.text: Architecture should never be an isolated review exercise; it must become an active operational capability that participates directly in software delivery. By shifting governance left into the software construction process itself while continuously reconciling repository evolution against architectural intent, CAS closes the gap between strategic direction and engineering reality. The Continuous Architecture System demonstrates that true operational sustainability emerges when architecture evolves from static documentation into a living, measurable, governance aware operational system capable of both preventing and correcting architectural entropy continuously.

docs.title: Institutional Patterns
docs.text:
Reusable architectural patterns approved by the Architecture Forum.

Pattern Classification Maturity Confidence README N/A N/A N/A Architecture Workflow Loop N/A exploring medium Build-Time Governance N/A exploring medium Contract-First Architecture N/A exploring medium Deterministic Core with Selective Augmentation N/A exploring medium Dual-Path Routing supporting exploring high Graceful Fallback N/A exploring high Heuristic-Driven Arbitration supporting exploring high LLM Provider Abstraction Boundary N/A exploring high Learning Loop Product Development N/A exploring medium Orchestrator-Managed Pipeline N/A exploring high Parallel Agent Execution N/A stable high Personal-Need Experimentation supporting exploring medium Prompt-as-Code Separation supporting exploring high Prompt–Schema Contracts as First-Class Architecture N/A exploring high Stabilise the Core Loop Before Adding Modalities supporting exploring medium Structured Output via Schema N/A exploring high Typed Execution Context N/A stable high

docs.title: Intent
docs.text: Ensure rapid experimentation does not create architectural debt by embedding an explicit architecture audit and refactor step directly inside the development workflow.

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
This pattern appears in experimentation-heavy systems where features evolve quickly and architectural drift becomes likely.

Observed in:

  • Iterative AI system development
  • Agent-based architectures
  • Continuous refactoring environments

The pressure emerges from a tension between fast feature iteration and long-term architectural stability.

Without an explicit governance mechanism, the system accumulates structural debt.

docs.title: Agentic profile
docs.text:
• System shape: multi-agent
  • Orchestration mode: hybrid

docs.title: Agent-to-agent interaction
docs.text:
• Present: true
  • Mechanism: shared-state
  • Evidence: src/core/pipeline/context.py#PipelineContext

docs.title: Tool protocols
docs.text:
• MCP: absent
  • Tool calling: present
  • Evidence: src/agents/insights/agent.py#generate_insights

docs.title: Optimisation target
docs.text:
• Primary: reliability
  • Secondary: developer-velocity
  • Notes: The pattern trades small development friction for long-term system stability.

docs.title: Simplicity vs autonomy
docs.text:
• Position: balanced
  • Rationale: The workflow preserves architectural discipline while still allowing rapid experimentation.

docs.title: Forces
docs.text:
• Rapid iteration introduces structural drift.
  • Refactors are often postponed when delivery pressure is high.
  • Agent-based architectures amplify coupling risks.
  • Architecture reviews are traditionally periodic rather than continuous.
  • Structural problems compound quickly if not addressed immediately.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
Introduce an architecture governance loop inside the delivery workflow.

Instead of merging changes directly after validation:

  1. Implement the change
  2. Validate behavior
  3. Run an architecture audit
  4. Refactor structural issues
  5. Stabilize and merge

This creates a continuous cycle:

Build → Validate → Audit → Refactor → Stabilize

The architecture audit becomes a mandatory checkpoint before merge, ensuring structural corrections happen immediately.

docs.title: Operationalisation (CAS Implementation)
docs.text: This pattern can be operationalised within CAS (Continuous Architecture System) by embedding governance capabilities directly into the delivery workflow.

docs.title: Mapping to CAS Components
docs.text:
• Build (Prevent)
  • CAS Skills guide code agents before implementation

  • Examples: select-pattern, define-contract, validate-against-rules

  • Validate

  • Standard test suites (unit, integration)

  • Schema and contract validation for structured outputs

  • Audit (Detect)

  • Automated audit via GitHub Actions or CI pipelines

  • Steps include:

    • Extraction of signals and patterns from code
    • Execution of rules-context-loader
    • Alignment Engine to compare AEM (state) vs AID (intent)

  • Refactor (Correct)

  • Audit outputs generate structured architecture_work
  • Architecture decisions are captured and updated via ADRs

  • Refactors are applied at architectural level, not just code level

  • Stabilize

  • Merge policies enforce governance checkpoints
  • Early stages: non-blocking audit with human-in-the-loop
  • Later stages: blocking conditions for critical violations

docs.title: Execution Flow
docs.text: Code Agent → CAS Skills → Code → CI Audit → Architecture Work → ADR → Refactor → Merge

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Prevention is lightweight and advisory; detection and correction provide stronger guarantees
  • Governance is applied via rule context (rules-context-loader) rather than embedded in prompts
  • This implementation enables continuous, automated architecture governance in agentic development workflows

docs.title: Implementation signals
docs.text:
Observable signals this pattern exists:

  • Explicit architecture audit step before merge
  • Refactor commits triggered by architectural violations
  • Workflow documentation referencing architecture checks
  • Audit-driven refactor phases during feature development
  • Architecture-focused test suites validating system structure

docs.title: Evidence
docs.text:
• runner-agentic-intelligence/src/agents/orchestrator.py — orchestration refactoring during pipeline migration
  • runner-agentic-intelligence/src/core/pipeline/context.py — typed PipelineContext introduced after architecture audit
  • Weekly learning artefacts documenting audit → refactor cycles
  • Architecture audit reports highlighting violations and structural corrections

docs.title: Benefits
docs.text:
• Prevents architecture drift during rapid experimentation
  • Surfaces structural problems early
  • Encourages disciplined refactoring
  • Improves long-term system maintainability

docs.title: Costs
docs.text:
• Slower merge cycle for individual changes
  • Additional cognitive overhead during development

docs.title: Failure modes
docs.text:
• Audits become superficial checklists instead of structural analysis
  • Teams bypass the audit step under delivery pressure
  • Refactor step becomes optional instead of mandatory

docs.title: Reuse notes
docs.text:
This pattern fits:

  • Systems evolving through continuous experimentation
  • AI and agent-based architectures
  • Small teams maintaining architecture discipline without formal review boards

This pattern does not fit well in:

  • Static systems with infrequent changes
  • Projects with heavy centralized architecture governance

Minimal version:

Build → Validate → Architecture Audit → Merge

Preconditions:

  • Team understands architectural boundaries
  • Audit criteria are clearly defined
  • Refactors are accepted as part of normal delivery

docs.title: Confidence
docs.text:
Medium

Observed clearly during multiple refactor cycles in the runner intelligence system, but limited validation across additional projects or teams.

⸻

docs.title: Intent
docs.text: Ensure invalid architectural structures cannot be produced by encoding architectural constraints directly into the generation process, rather than relying on post-hoc validation and refactoring.

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
This pattern applies to systems where code or structure is generated dynamically, particularly:

  • AI agent-driven development workflows
  • Code generation pipelines
  • Automated architectural scaffolding
  • Multi-agent systems producing structural outputs

The context is characterized by rapid iteration where architectural drift is likely if constraints are not enforced proactively.

docs.title: Problem
docs.text:
When agents generate code or system structures without constraint enforcement:

  • Invalid architectural structures are produced and then must be detected and refactored
  • Post-hoc validation leads to structural drift and inconsistency
  • Manual review becomes a bottleneck
  • Architectural standards degrade over time as generated code accumulates
  • Refactoring cost compounds with each generation cycle

docs.title: Forces
docs.text:
• Speed of generation vs. correctness of output
  • Flexibility for agents vs. architectural control
  • Development velocity vs. long-term system stability
  • Automation vs. human oversight

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
Encode architectural constraints into the generation process so invalid structures cannot be produced in the first place:

  1. Constraint Definition: Define architectural rules as explicit constraints (schemas, templates, validation rules)

  2. Agent Skill Integration: Embed constraints into agent skills and prompt instructions

  3. Generation-Time Enforcement: Apply constraints during code/structure generation, not after

  4. Feedback Loop: Provide immediate feedback to agents when constraints would be violated

Implementation approaches:

  • Use structured output schemas (JSON, Pydantic) that agents must conform to
  • Define agent skills with built-in architectural guardrails
  • Create templates with constrained injection points
  • Implement generation pipelines that validate at each stage

docs.title: Implementation Signals
docs.text:
• Schema validation applied during generation, not after
  • Agent prompts include explicit architectural constraints
  • Agent skills restrict outputs to valid architectural patterns
  • Contract enforcement embedded in prompts or pipelines
  • Generated code requires no structural refactoring
  • Templates used for consistent structure generation

docs.title: Evidence
docs.text:
• threads/architecture-signals-retrospective.v7: Build-time architectural enforcement via agent constraints
  • Pattern observed: "Prevent structural violations through agent-enforced guardrails before code is produced"
  • Anti-pattern identified: "Relying solely on post-build audit/refactor to maintain architecture"
  • Heuristic: "If code is generated by agents, architecture must be encoded as constraints, not documentation"

docs.title: Consequences
docs.text:
Benefits: - Prevents structural drift at the source - Reduces refactoring overhead - Improves architectural consistency across generated code - Makes architectural standards machine-enforceable - Reduces manual review burden for structural concerns

Trade-offs / Limitations: - Requires upfront design effort to define constraints - May limit agent flexibility in legitimate edge cases - Constraint maintenance adds ongoing overhead - Over-constrained systems may become rigid
