docs.title: Initial Discovery Flow and Alignment Baseline
Initial Discovery Flow and Alignment Baseline
Problem Statement
docs.text:
The baseline assessment identifies that the repository has strong foundational assets, including operational guidance, CAS skill definitions, prompt contracts, and knowledge templates. However, governance evidence has not yet been fully established from those assets.

The current state is template-ready rather than operationally governed. Architecture Work, Alignment Reports, Architecture Evolution Maps, and Architecture Intent evidence need to be produced through the lifecycle rather than remaining implicit in templates and guidance.

docs.title: Why This Matters
docs.text:
Without an executed discovery and alignment baseline, CAS cannot reliably distinguish intended architecture from observed architecture. Drift detection, governance readiness assessment, and lifecycle progression all depend on populated governance evidence.

This gap affects governance maturity because downstream artifacts such as AEMs and AIDs need source Architecture Work and alignment evidence to summarize.

docs.title: Expected Outcomes
docs.text:
The desired future state is an initialized governance baseline for ideas-to-life-cas that records the first cycle of discovery, Architecture Work, and alignment evidence.

Success indicators include populated Architecture Work, alignment evidence that can be summarized by governance artifacts, and clear traceability from baseline discovery findings into the architecture backlog.

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text:
• Baseline Discovery
  • Architecture Work Registry
  • Alignment Engine
  • Architecture Evolution Maps
  • Architecture Intent Documents

docs.title: Discovery Evidence
docs.text:
The baseline assessment states that governance templates are well-defined but populated instances are absent from the provided asset list, indicating a pre-execution or bootstrap state.

It also identifies the candidate to execute the initial discovery flow to generate first Architecture Work and Alignment Report.

docs.title: Recommended Next Step
docs.text: Run the initial discovery-to-alignment lifecycle for ideas-to-life-cas and use the resulting evidence to establish the first governed architecture baseline.

docs.title: Initial ADR for the Core Orchestration Pipeline
Initial ADR for the Core Orchestration Pipeline
Problem Statement
docs.text:
The baseline assessment identifies orchestration flows and alignment engines as structurally ready, but it does not identify a populated Architecture Decision Record capturing the core orchestration pipeline as a governed architectural decision.

The current state leaves a foundational runtime and governance choice implicit in repository structure, prompt contracts, and operational documentation.

docs.title: Why This Matters
docs.text:
The orchestration pipeline is a central architectural capability for CAS. If its role is not captured as an ADR, future changes may alter the pipeline without a clear decision record, rationale, or governance trail.

This affects governance because architecture evolution depends on stable decision provenance for core runtime and workflow boundaries.

docs.title: Expected Outcomes
docs.text:
The desired future state is a populated ADR that records the core orchestration pipeline decision, explains why CAS uses the pipeline as a governing execution model, and establishes traceability to the baseline assessment.

Success indicators include a decision record with clear status, rationale, affected components, and links to related Architecture Work and governance artifacts.

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text:
• Governance Foundation
  • Architecture Decision Records
  • Core orchestration pipeline
  • Alignment Engine

docs.title: Discovery Evidence
docs.text:
The baseline assessment identifies the candidate to create an initial Architecture Decision Record for the core orchestration pipeline.

It also concludes that orchestration flows and alignment engines are structurally ready but require actual architecture work and decision records to mature.

docs.title: Recommended Next Step
docs.text: Promote the core orchestration pipeline decision into an ADR candidate and route it through the project governance lifecycle.

docs.title: Problem Statement
docs.text:
The baseline assessment identifies well-defined knowledge layer templates for ADRs, AEMs, AIDs, and Architecture Work, but it also identifies a need to validate and populate those templates with project-specific patterns and rules.

The current state provides reusable governance structure, but the project-specific governance substrate needs evidence that it reflects the actual architecture constraints of ideas-to-life-cas.

docs.title: Why This Matters
docs.text:
Template readiness is not the same as project governance readiness. CAS needs populated and validated knowledge artifacts so generated outputs remain grounded in the project architecture rather than generic template structure.

This affects architecture quality because unvalidated templates can create a false sense of governance maturity while leaving project-specific constraints implicit.

docs.title: Expected Outcomes
docs.text:
The desired future state is a validated project knowledge layer where templates, patterns, rules, and generated artifacts are populated with ideas-to-life-cas specific architectural constraints.

Success indicators include project-specific knowledge artifacts, validated frontmatter contracts, and clear relationships between templates, patterns, rules, and generated governance evidence.

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text:
• Knowledge Provider
  • Governance Foundation
  • Architecture Work Registry
  • Project-specific patterns and rules

docs.title: Discovery Evidence
docs.text:
The baseline assessment identifies the candidate to validate and populate knowledge layer templates with project-specific patterns and rules.

It also notes that the knowledge layer templates enforce strict frontmatter and schema contracts supporting deterministic derivation.

docs.title: Recommended Next Step
docs.text: Review the project knowledge templates against current ideas-to-life-cas architecture constraints and populate the first project-specific governance artifacts needed for deterministic lifecycle execution.

docs.title: Automated CI Validation for CAS Skill Enforcement
Automated CI Validation for CAS Skill Enforcement
Problem Statement
docs.text:
The baseline assessment identifies mandatory CAS skill usage as an architectural governance concern and proposes automated CI validation for CAS skill enforcement.

The current state depends on contributor and agent discipline to preserve the governed workflow, but discovery indicates an opportunity to make enforcement visible and repeatable through automation.

docs.title: Why This Matters
docs.text:
CAS skill enforcement protects the repository from architectural drift by ensuring changes pass through governed workflows. Without automation, enforcement can become inconsistent across contributors, agents, and pull requests.

This affects governance operations because architectural guardrails should be verifiable as part of the delivery lifecycle rather than relying only on manual review.

docs.title: Expected Outcomes
docs.text:
The desired future state is CI validation that makes CAS skill enforcement observable during repository changes.

Success indicators include an automated check tied to governed workflow expectations, clear failure output for non-compliant changes, and reduced reliance on manual detection of skill bypasses.

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text:
• GovernanceOps
  • CAS Skill Enforcement
  • CI/CD workflow governance
  • Architecture Work Registry

docs.title: Discovery Evidence
docs.text:
The baseline assessment identifies the candidate to implement automated CI validation for CAS skill enforcement.

It states that this would prevent architectural drift by ensuring all code changes pass through mandatory CAS skills.

docs.title: Recommended Next Step
docs.text: Define the CI-level governance signal for CAS skill enforcement and route the proposed automation through the repository's managed steering and validation workflow.

docs.title: Vision Driver
docs.text: The future vision expands CAS from a decision-driven architecture system into an Architecture Intelligence Platform that connects business intent, architecture, code, and decisions. It specifically calls for ingestion of business requirements, business cases, portfolio definitions, and strategic objectives, then tracing business requirements through ADRs, patterns, and code.

docs.title: Current State
docs.text: CAS has an emerging Architecture Work Registry and a knowledge layer for architecture work, decisions, patterns, maps, and intent. The current diagrams show business stakeholders providing intent and requirements to CAS, but the project does not yet define a governed capability for extracting business intent signals or linking those signals through the architecture knowledge model.

docs.title: Desired Future State
docs.text: CAS should represent business intent as governed architecture knowledge that can be traced to patterns, ADRs, architecture work, implementation structure, and runtime behavior. Architects should be able to ask which architectural decisions support a business capability and receive evidence-backed traceability.

docs.title: Evolution Gap
docs.text: The gap is a missing business-intent model, ingestion workflow, and traceability relationship contract. Current knowledge artifacts can hold architecture evidence, but they do not yet provide a first-class path from business inputs to architecture decisions and downstream implementation evidence.

docs.title: Why This Matters
docs.text: Business intent integration gives CAS strategic relevance beyond architecture documentation. Architecturally, it anchors decisions in business drivers. From a governance perspective, it improves decision justification and review context. Operationally, it makes change impact easier to reason about across business, architecture, and implementation layers.

docs.title: Expected Outcomes
docs.text:
• Business intent signals become governed architecture inputs.
  • Architecture Work and ADRs can reference business drivers explicitly.
  • Traceability queries can connect business requirements to patterns, decisions, and code evidence.
  • Governance review gains clearer evidence for why architectural choices matter.

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text:
• Architecture Work Registry
  • Knowledge Provider
  • ADR lifecycle
  • Pattern relationship model
  • Future code intelligence capability

docs.title: Vision Evidence
docs.text: The vision asks how to connect business intent to architecture and implementation, defines Business Layer Integration as a capability domain, and identifies end-to-end traceability from business to decision, pattern, code, and runtime behavior as a cross-cutting capability.

docs.title: Recommended Next Step
docs.text: Define the minimal business intent knowledge model and identify the first governed ingestion workflow that can convert business artifacts into Architecture Work without introducing implementation design.

docs.title: Vision Driver
docs.text: The future vision calls for CAS to move from pattern extraction toward full code understanding and continuous alignment between intended architecture and implementation reality.

docs.title: Current State
docs.text: The current execution sequence and orchestration diagrams show code repositories, CI, rule context loading, alignment analysis, and architecture work generation. These flows establish a path for operational governance, but code intelligence remains primarily represented as future structural analysis, deterministic extraction, and alignment signals rather than a mature capability.

docs.title: Desired Future State
docs.text: CAS should understand codebase structure, identify boundaries and dependencies, map implementation evidence to patterns and architecture intent, and detect where code deviates from AID, ADRs, or governed patterns. The capability should support both prevention and detection of architectural drift.

docs.title: Evolution Gap
docs.text: The gap is a governed code intelligence model that can translate repository structure into architecture evidence. Current workflows can run and persist governance artifacts, but they do not yet provide a complete architectural representation of modules, dependencies, boundaries, drift, and fit/gap recommendations.

docs.title: Why This Matters
docs.text: Code intelligence is foundational to closing the loop between architecture intent and delivery. Architecturally, it grounds AID and AEM claims in implementation evidence. Governance improves because drift becomes visible earlier. Operationally, teams can implement features and refactors with stronger architectural context.

docs.title: Expected Outcomes
docs.text:
• Code structure is represented as governed architecture evidence.
  • Alignment checks compare AID, ADRs, patterns, and code reality.
  • Drift findings produce Architecture Work rather than remaining isolated reports.
  • CI and delivery workflows gain evidence-backed prevention and detection signals.

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text:
• Architecture Work Registry
  • GovernanceOps
  • AID and AEM lifecycle
  • Rule context loading
  • CI/CD validation model

docs.title: Vision Evidence
docs.text: The vision identifies code structural analysis, architectural boundary detection, code-to-pattern mapping, AID-vs-code comparison, and automated fit/gap analysis as required future capabilities.

docs.title: Recommended Next Step
docs.text: Define the first code intelligence evidence model and the governance boundary for how code-derived findings become Architecture Work, alignment findings, or CI validation signals.

docs.title: Operational Intelligence Loop for ReasoningOps and GovernanceOps
Operational Intelligence Loop for ReasoningOps and GovernanceOps
Vision Driver
docs.text: The future vision positions ReasoningOps and GovernanceOps as continuously participating operational capabilities that convert conversations, implementation activity, architecture work, and governance signals into architectural intelligence.

docs.title: Current State
docs.text: The project has established Governance Foundation v1, governance-aware orchestration diagrams, and architecture work for CI skill enforcement. The orchestration flow also includes operational intelligence as a participant. However, operational intelligence is not yet defined as a complete lifecycle that consistently evaluates signals, recommends architecture evolution, and feeds governance refinement.

docs.title: Desired Future State
docs.text: CAS should continuously evaluate alignment between intent, implementation, patterns, decisions, and system state. It should surface governance drift, emerging patterns, capability evolution, and prioritized architecture work while keeping human governance authoritative.

docs.title: Evolution Gap
docs.text: The gap is a defined operational intelligence loop with clear signal sources, reasoning responsibilities, governance outputs, lifecycle states, and trust calibration. Current governance participation exists, but the future operating model requires a repeatable prevent, detect, and feedback loop.

docs.title: Why This Matters
docs.text: ReasoningOps and GovernanceOps turn governance from periodic review into lightweight participation in delivery. Architecturally, this improves the system's ability to reason about itself. Governance becomes adaptive because execution evidence can refine patterns and decisions. Operationally, teams receive earlier and more relevant architecture guidance.

docs.title: Expected Outcomes
docs.text:
• Operational signals consistently produce architecture intelligence.
  • Governance drift and emerging patterns are routed into Architecture Work.
  • Reasoning loops support prioritization of architecture evolution.
  • Human review remains the authority for accepting decisions and governance changes.

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text:
• Governance Foundation
  • Architecture Work Registry
  • CI/CD governance signals
  • Alignment Engine
  • Operational intelligence artifact model

docs.title: Vision Evidence
docs.text: The vision describes ReasoningOps and GovernanceOps as capabilities for continuous evaluation, architecture evolution recommendations, governance drift detection, pattern refinement, trust calibration, and operational participation throughout delivery.

docs.title: Recommended Next Step
docs.text: Define the operational intelligence artifact contract and map how prevent, detect, and feedback signals flow into Architecture Work, ADRs, pattern updates, and alignment reports.

docs.title: Vision Driver
docs.text: The future vision positions CAS within enterprise architecture ecosystems and calls for a plugin or ingestion framework for BRD parsers, code analyzers, external system connectors, and knowledge platform integrations.

docs.title: Current State
docs.text: CAS currently has local knowledge artifacts, prompt-based workflows, runner automation, and diagrams showing LLM providers, CI/CD, code repositories, and runtime systems as adjacent participants. External enterprise architecture tools, source-control integrations beyond local repository workflows, and knowledge-platform synchronization remain future-facing.

docs.title: Desired Future State
docs.text: CAS should ingest and synchronize architecture knowledge across enterprise architecture management tools, knowledge platforms, source control systems, and CI/CD pipelines. New input types should follow a standard ingestion interface so external evidence can become governed Architecture Work, ADRs, patterns, maps, and intent.

docs.title: Evolution Gap
docs.text: The gap is a stable integration boundary and provider model for external architecture evidence. Current workflows are mostly file and prompt oriented; the future state requires governed connectors, synchronization rules, provenance handling, and lifecycle integration for external systems.

docs.title: Why This Matters
docs.text: External ecosystem integration prevents CAS from becoming an isolated repository-local architecture tool. Architecturally, it establishes clean boundaries for external providers. Governance improves because decisions and patterns can synchronize across systems. Operationally, teams can bring existing enterprise evidence into CAS without manual duplication.

docs.title: Expected Outcomes
docs.text:
• External inputs follow a standard ingestion and provenance model.
  • Enterprise tools and knowledge platforms can contribute governed architecture evidence.
  • CAS can export validated decisions and patterns to external repositories.
  • Synchronization responsibilities are explicit and governable.

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text:
• Knowledge Provider
  • Business Intent Integration
  • Architecture Work Registry
  • Provenance and source tracking conventions
  • Future connector contracts

docs.title: Vision Evidence
docs.text: The vision identifies enterprise architecture management tools, knowledge platforms, source control systems, CI/CD pipelines, external artifact ingestion, decision synchronization, and plugin-based input interfaces as target capabilities.

docs.title: Recommended Next Step
docs.text: Define the external ingestion provider contract and governance rules for provenance, synchronization direction, conflict handling, and Architecture Work creation.

docs.title: Interactive Architecture Intelligence Maps and Knowledge Graph
Interactive Architecture Intelligence Maps and Knowledge Graph
Vision Driver
docs.text: The future vision calls for an interactive Architecture Intelligence Map, decision flow visualization, pattern relationship graph, timeline of architecture evolution, and a future knowledge graph layer for complex queries.

docs.title: Current State
docs.text: CAS has static diagram artifacts, architecture maps, intent documents, and relationship-bearing frontmatter. These provide architectural visibility, but exploration remains document-centric rather than graph-centric or interactive.

docs.title: Desired Future State
docs.text: CAS should provide an explorable architecture intelligence layer where architects can navigate decisions, patterns, architecture work, maps, intent, code evidence, and business intent as connected knowledge. The same relationship model should support visual exploration and structured queries.

docs.title: Evolution Gap
docs.text: The gap is a first-class relationship graph model and interaction layer. Current artifacts contain relationships, but they are not yet normalized into a governed graph that can power visualization, timelines, impact analysis, or cross-project queries.

docs.title: Why This Matters
docs.text: Interactive architecture intelligence makes CAS usable as an exploration and reasoning platform, not only a document generator. Architecturally, it reveals relationships and change impact. Governance improves because decision lineage and pattern evolution become easier to inspect. Operationally, it reduces the cost of understanding why the system evolved.

docs.title: Expected Outcomes
docs.text:
• CAS knowledge relationships are normalized into a graph-ready model.
  • Architects can explore decision flow, pattern relationships, and evolution timelines.
  • Cross-artifact and cross-project queries become possible.
  • Visualization reflects governed source artifacts rather than becoming a separate source of truth.

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text:
• Knowledge Provider
  • Architecture Work Registry
  • Business Intent Integration
  • Code Intelligence
  • Relationship and provenance conventions

docs.title: Vision Evidence
docs.text: The vision identifies AIM, decision flow visualization, pattern relationship graphs, evolution timelines, and knowledge graph queries as future capabilities for making architecture explorable and interactive.

docs.title: Recommended Next Step
docs.text: Define the governed relationship model for Architecture Work, ADRs, patterns, AEM, AID, diagrams, business intent, and code evidence before selecting visualization or graph storage mechanisms.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Establishment of an automated 'Detect-Analyse-Execute' loop to operationalise architectural governance.

docs.title: Details
docs.text: This work item focuses on moving from manual alignment reporting to a closed-loop governance system. It involves integrating the Alignment Engine with CI/CD pipelines to automatically detect drift, analyse impact using the CAS reasoning model, and propose or execute remediation workflows.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: Proposed: Implement a runtime-native governance controller that orchestrates the detection and analysis phases without human intervention for non-breaking drift.

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text: The current governance velocity ratio (15:1) indicates that manual review is a bottleneck. Automation of the governance loop is required to maintain architectural integrity at scale.

docs.title: Implementation Plan
docs.text: 1. Define CI-based triggers for alignment checks. 2. Develop the 'Analyse' workflow to consume alignment reports. 3. Integrate with the Architecture Work Registry to auto-generate 'proposed' items for detected drift.

docs.title: Alternatives Considered
docs.text: ['Maintain manual review cycles (rejected due to velocity bottleneck)', 'Purely deterministic rule enforcement (rejected as it lacks the reasoning capability required for complex patterns)']

docs.title: Risks
docs.text: ['High volume of automated work items could overwhelm the registry', 'False positives in drift detection could lead to unnecessary refactoring']

docs.title: Expected Outcomes
docs.text: Reduction in governance velocity bottleneck; 100% visibility of architectural drift in real-time; automated provenance for all governance-driven changes.

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text: Alignment Engine, Architecture Work Registry, CAS Workflow Execution Model

docs.title: Recommended Next Step
docs.text: Define the semantic contract for 'Automated Remediation' signals within the Alignment Engine.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Development of an Architecture Knowledge Graph for cross-project relationship modeling and lineage analysis.

docs.title: Details
docs.text: The vision identifies a need for an 'Architecture Intelligence Layer'. This work item involves transitioning from isolated project-level artifacts to a unified graph-based representation of architectural knowledge, enabling complex queries across projects and time.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: Proposed: Adopt a graph-based schema for representing relationships between ADRs, Patterns, Signals, and Code artifacts.

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text: Current artifacts are siloed. A Knowledge Graph enables the system to reason about the impact of a pattern change in one project on the reliability of another, fulfilling the 'Continuous Intelligence' vision.

docs.title: Implementation Plan
docs.text: 1. Define the Graph Schema (Nodes: Decision, Pattern, Signal, Artifact). 2. Implement Knowledge Providers for graph ingestion. 3. Develop query interfaces for the Alignment Engine.

docs.title: Alternatives Considered
docs.text: ['Relational database storage (rejected due to the complexity of recursive relationship traversal)', 'Enhanced flat-file indexing (rejected as insufficient for multi-hop lineage analysis)']

docs.title: Risks
docs.text: ['Complexity of maintaining graph consistency across distributed projects', 'Performance overhead of graph traversal during runtime reasoning']

docs.title: Expected Outcomes
docs.text: Ability to perform cross-project impact analysis; visual lineage of architectural decisions; improved signal-to-pattern promotion accuracy.

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text: Knowledge Provider Abstraction, Architecture Evolution Map

docs.title: Recommended Next Step
docs.text: Draft the initial graph schema for Decision-Pattern-Signal relationships.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Integration of Business Intent into the architectural reasoning loop to ensure strategic alignment.

docs.title: Details
docs.text: This work item bridges the gap between high-level business vision and architectural execution. It involves creating a formal mechanism for the system to ingest 'Business Intent' artifacts and use them as constraints during the 'Analyse' phase of the governance loop.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: Proposed: Formalise the 'Business Intent' artifact schema and integrate it as a mandatory input for the Alignment Engine.

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text: The vision emphasizes 'connecting business intent to code reality'. Without this integration, architectural governance remains focused on technical consistency rather than strategic value.

docs.title: Implementation Plan
docs.text: 1. Define the Business Intent Schema. 2. Update the Alignment Engine to weight 'Intent Alignment' as a high-severity metric. 3. Create a 'VisionToIntent' workflow to automate artifact generation.

docs.title: Alternatives Considered
docs.text: ['Manual mapping of intent to architecture (rejected as non-scalable)', 'Treating intent as purely narrative documentation (rejected as it prevents automated alignment validation)']

docs.title: Risks
docs.text: ['Ambiguity in business intent could lead to conflicting architectural constraints', "Potential for 'intent drift' where business goals change faster than architecture can evolve"]

docs.title: Expected Outcomes
docs.text: Traceability from code to business value; automated detection of 'Strategic Drift'; improved prioritisation of Architecture Work based on business impact.

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text: Alignment Engine, Vision Artifacts

docs.title: Recommended Next Step
docs.text: Conduct a pilot mapping of the 'CAS 2026 Vision' to the proposed Business Intent Schema.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Governance Portal Implementation

docs.title: Details
docs.text: Implementation of a published governance experience using MkDocs and GitHub Pages to move beyond Obsidian-based visualization. The portal includes navigation for Home, Projects, Architecture, Governance, Intelligence, and Operations.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Alignment Summary Frontmatter Promotion

docs.title: Details
docs.text: Promote alignment summary values (status, issue counts) from markdown text into Alignment Report frontmatter.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Knowledge Provider Abstraction

docs.title: Details
docs.text: Introduction of the Knowledge Provider abstraction to decouple workflows from repository structure. Candidate Signal: Knowledge Access Abstraction (Insight: Workflows should remain implementation agnostic regarding storage).

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: CAS Workflow Execution Model

docs.title: Details
docs.text: Establishment of a standardized model where Runner orchestrates, Workflow reasons, Knowledge Provider provides knowledge, and Prompts are contracts. Candidate Signal: Workflow Execution Model Compliance (Insight: Runtime-native workflows must separate reasoning from orchestration).

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Architecture Work Runtime Capability Selection

docs.title: Details
docs.text: Selection of Option A: Independent workflows (conversation-to-aw, discovery-to-aw, vision-to-aw) for runtime Architecture Work generation.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Runtime Onboarding Program

docs.title: Details
docs.text: Conversion of prompt-native workflows into runtime-native capabilities (ConversationToAW, DiscoveryToAW, VisionToAW) integrated with ReasoningExecutionContract.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Continuous Architecture Governance Runtime

docs.title: Details
docs.text: A proposed capability to automate the lifecycle: Detect -> Analyse -> Plan -> Execute -> Publish -> Observe. This ensures intelligence freshness without manual execution.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Architecture Intelligence Layer

docs.title: Details
docs.text: A future layer responsible for signal detection, pattern discovery, pattern promotion recommendations, and cross-project learning.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Portfolio Intelligence Capability

docs.title: Details
docs.text: Expansion of CAS to provide cross-project architecture visibility, shared governance intelligence, and strategic architecture trends.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Architecture Knowledge Graph

docs.title: Details
docs.text: A unified architecture intelligence model for relationship modelling, lineage, and capability dependency analysis.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Operational Governance Surface

docs.title: Details
docs.text: Evolution of the Governance Portal into a real-time operational view of architecture governance for daily use.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: The CAS platform is evolving from a documentation and manual governance model into a Continuous Architecture Governance platform. This work captures the evolution of the Governance Portal, Pattern Governance Lifecycle, Workflow Execution Model, Governance Intelligence/Metrics, Compliance Engine, and the strategic vision for a Runtime-native Governance layer.

docs.title: Details
docs.text: Covers Governance Portal evolution (Sprints 1-3), Pattern Governance lifecycle institutionalization, Workflow Execution Model compliance, Governance Intelligence domain modeling, Traceable Metrics, Artifact Reference Resolver, Rules vs Policies decision, Governance Compliance & Exception framework, Observability fixes, Governance Action Framework design, and Strategic Vision for Continuous Governance Runtime.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: Institutionalize a formal Governance Lifecycle (Proposal -> Promotion Recommendation -> Promotion Decision -> ADR -> Pattern). Adopt a unified Workflow Execution Model where CAS Runner orchestrates and workflows reason. Select independent workflows over a unified generator for Architecture Work. Establish Rules as the authoritative governance constraint source over separate policies. Introduce a Governance Action Framework for state changes.

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text: Manual execution caused intelligence staleness and architecture drift. A standardized execution model ensures consistency. Formalizing the lifecycle prevents governance state leakage into proposal artifacts. Rules provide a flexible, auditable compliance engine. The Action Framework addresses the gap in human approval automation.

docs.title: Implementation Plan
docs.text: 1. Complete Workflow Onboarding Program for conversation/discovery/vision-to-aw. 2. Implement Governance Action Engine and CLI/Portal interfaces. 3. Deploy Governance Compliance Engine with CI/CD integration. 4. Develop Traceable Metrics and Artifact Reference Resolver. 5. Evolve Portal to Architecture Intelligence Portal. 6. Future: Implement Continuous Pattern Intelligence and Portfolio Intelligence.

docs.title: Alternatives Considered
docs.text: ['Option A (Independent workflows) vs Option B (Unified Architecture Work Generator) -> Selected A for lower risk and validation of execution model.', 'Separate Policy Hierarchy vs Existing Rule Framework -> Selected Rules as authoritative source.', 'Markdown-only artifacts vs Structured JSON/YAML -> Adopted structured schemas for machine consumption.']

docs.title: Risks
docs.text: ['Intelligence freshness remains dependent on workflow execution frequency.', 'Data fragmentation across conversations, ADRs, AEMs, and external tools (Jira, GitHub Projects).', 'Manual approval bottlenecks in current governance actions.', 'Potential over-engineering of the Knowledge Graph layer.']

docs.title: Expected Outcomes
docs.text: Reduced architecture drift through continuous governance. Machine-consumable governance artifacts. Runtime-native workflow execution. Improved portal navigation and drill-down capabilities. Clear separation of governance state and actions. Foundation for automated pattern discovery and portfolio intelligence.

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text: CAS Runner orchestration, Knowledge Provider abstraction, ReasoningExecutionContract, GitHub Actions automation, MkDocs/GitHub Pages portal infrastructure.

docs.title: Recommended Next Step
docs.text: Finalize and onboard remaining prompt-native workflows to the CAS Runner. Implement the Governance Action Framework to automate approval/rejection flows. Begin prototyping the Architecture Knowledge Graph for cross-project intelligence.
